Summary:
The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through several national channels. The Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Innovations has shared it as part of its policy coordination mandate, while the Agency for Innovative Development has incorporated open science aspects into its projects. The Center for Scientific and Technical Information has supported awareness-building campaigns and educational programmes. Research institutions and universities have also engaged in discussions and training sessions aimed at integrating the Recommendation's principles into national strategies.

 Awareness-raising activities on open science have been organised and are planned through to 2025. The Ministry and the Agency have conducted workshops and training sessions for researchers, academics and policymakers covering open science principles, benefits and implementation strategies. National conferences and forums have facilitated discussion among scientists, universities and research institutions on open science practices and challenges. Informational publications and online resources are being developed to reach a wider audience.

Uzbekistan's national science, technology and innovation policy is the Strategy for the Innovative Development of the Republic of Uzbekistan for 2022–2026, approved by Presidential Decree No. PF-165 on 6 July 2022. Developed by the Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Innovations, the strategy prioritises innovative infrastructure, state support for innovative activity, regional innovation in small businesses and human capital in innovation management. The national legal framework addressing open science is described as only partly in place, with this decree as the principal instrument. Specific policy instruments to promote open science are reported as under development; no dedicated instruments yet exist, though the strategy indirectly supports open science values through its emphasis on collaboration and knowledge sharing.


 A specific national funding mechanism for open science is established through Cabinet of Ministers Resolution No. 133 (9 March 2020) “On Measures to Further Improve the Regulatory and Legal Framework for the Development of Scientific Research and Innovation Activities”. The same resolution is cited as the basis for integrating open science practices into existing research funding.

 Efforts to promote equitable public-private partnerships are set out in several presidential decrees. Decree No. PF-6097 (29 October 2020) targets raising the private sector's share of total science funding to 20 per cent by 2025. Both Decree No. PF-165 and Decree No. PF-16 (30 January 2025) set targets for private sector investment in science and innovation of 6.0 billion soums. However, the information provided in the report has not clarified whether open science is explicitly integrated into these initiatives.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is planned for implementation under above-mentioned Resolution No. 133.

National open science infrastructures are reported as planned rather than operational; the report states that these will be implemented under Cabinet of Ministers Resolution No. 133 (9 March 2020).

 Uzbekistan participates in the Central Asian Network of Science and Education (CASNET), a federated regional infrastructure supporting high-performance computing, cloud services and data sharing for universities and research institutions across Central Asia.

 The report also describes South-South collaborations through CASNET and other regional initiatives to promote shared open science platforms, facilitating research and data exchange with other countries.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research-performing institutions through training programmes, workshops and seminars organised by the Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Innovations, the Agency for Innovative Development and universities. These address open science principles, data sharing and collaborative research practices.

 Capacity building programmes for policymakers have also been conducted. The National Training Workshop on Science, Technology and Innovation Park Policies and Practices for Sustainable Development aimed to build policymakers' capacity in designing and implementing relevant policies. The Modernizing Uzbekistan's National Innovation System (MUNIS) project, supported by the World Bank, focuses on strengthening the capacity of public research organisations to achieve international research quality standards and enable the commercialisation of research results. However, the information provided in the report has not clarified whether open science is explicitly integrated into these initiatives.

 Educational institutions have begun to incorporate a framework of open science competencies into higher education research skills curricula, including training on data sharing, open-access publishing and collaborative research. Broader frameworks are expected to be fully integrated into university curricula by 2025.

 Open educational resources are used for open science capacity building. The Modernisation of Higher Education in Central Asia through New Technologies (HiEdTec) project developed a virtual library of Uzbekistan offering digital educational resources on the Moodle learning management system. A separate project on capacity building for digital pedagogy offers hybrid courses titled "The Digital Teacher" for teacher training.

Incorporation of specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems is reported as planned under Cabinet of Ministers Resolution No. 133 (9 March 2020).

 Regarding initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices, the Concept of Development of Science until 2030 outlines strategies including a national rating system to evaluate the effectiveness of scientific and innovative activities, aimed at improving partnerships between the state and scientific organisations. The government also plans to raise the share of private sector funding for research and development from 8 per cent to 20 per cent by 2025, and to 30 per cent by 2030, which the report describes as intended to incentivise open science practices.
The information provided in the report does not directly address the questions covered by this section.

Uzbekistan promotes international scientific collaborations on open science through several channels. In October 2023, Uzbekistan and China signed a Memorandum of Understanding to establish an Open Science Framework, aiming to improve interoperability between their scientific data platforms and facilitate open sharing of scientific data.

Uzbekistan is involved in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science. It participates in UNESCO initiatives that include discussions on global open science funding. As a member of the Economic Cooperation Organization (ECO) Science Foundation, an intergovernmental body promoting scientific and technological research among its member states, Uzbekistan engages in initiatives that may encompass open science funding discussions.

The Committee for Open Science (Comité pour la Science Ouverte), active since 2018, has drawn on the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science to support national policy and disseminates it through its website.

 Awareness-raising has been conducted primarily through the second National Plan for Open Science, which contains over one hundred measures. A large proportion of these have been completed; those not yet realised due to time constraints are planned for the near future. The actions focus on two of the Recommendation's pillars: open scientific knowledge and open science infrastructures.

France's science, technology and innovation policy, expressed through the France 2030 acceleration strategies comprising 20 thematic national strategies, references the National Plan for Open Science, addressing open access and open science infrastructures.

 The principal legal framework is the Law for a Digital Republic (Loi pour une République Numérique, 2016), which frames the opening and sharing of research data and publications; the Committee for Open Science provides an application guide. From 2018, two successive national plans have further detailed policy, while the Committee for Open Science serves as the governance instrument, led under the Ministry of Higher Education and Research and including funding and evaluation agencies, national research organisations and universities, organised in a steering committee and thematic colleges. The second National Plan covers four axes: open publications, research data sharing, software and source code sharing and generalising practices through evaluation reform; officially ending in 2024, completion is expected by at least 2025.

 

At the institutional level, approximately 60% of French institutions (61 establishments) have adopted institutional open science policies, tracked through the French Open Science Barometer.

 A National Fund for Open Science (Fonds national pour la science ouverte) has operated since 2019 as a dedicated funding mechanism for open science. The National Research Agency (Agence Nationale de la Recherche) requires immediate open access for articles from funded projects, though open science has not been integrated into all funding mechanisms. No policy specifically targets public-private partnerships in support of open science. The Open Science Barometer (Baromètre de la science ouverte), built from open data, is the national monitoring framework, providing indicators on publications, data, software, theses, clinical trials, observational studies and institutional policies.

France operates several national non-profit open science infrastructures. HAL (Hyper Articles en Ligne), created in 2001, hosts scientific documents, commonly preprints, and complies fully with FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) principles via OAI-PMH (Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting), while also adhering to POSI (Principles of Open Scholarly Infrastructure) and CoreTrustSeal. Recherche Data Gouv, inaugurated in 2022, federates data workshops distributed across the territory, thematic resource and reference centres, and a general-purpose research data repository for communities lacking disciplinary repositories; it is FAIR-compliant by design. Software Heritage, launched in 2016 and supported by INRIA (Institut National de Recherche en Informatique et en Automatique) and UNESCO, archives software and source code and is FAIR-compliant by construction. OpenEdition and the Centre Mersenne are non-profit diamond open access platforms for journals and books. A broader ecosystem of software forges is documented in a dedicated report. The national research infrastructure strategy includes a detailed compliance analysis of all research infrastructures against open science policy.

 These principal infrastructures also function at international scale. HAL, with interfaces in French and English, is open to researchers worldwide. Recherche Data Gouv is accessible across Europe. Software Heritage serves developers globally across public and private sectors.

 France does not participate in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations for shared open science platforms.

Systematic capacity building on open science has not been undertaken at the national level. Higher education and research institutions are sovereign, and no national body holds direct prerogatives in this area; institutions are, however, strongly encouraged to strengthen their capacities, and a public survey has documented their training activities. No formal training programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies exist. Instead, policymaker engagement takes the form of targeted interventions on specific open science themes at national community meetings, described as debates rather than formal training.

 No national framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula. The report describes this as premature, noting that studies on a competency framework for research data management are underway but that no sufficiently consensual vision yet exists.

 Open educational resources are used for capacity building. A number of these have been created by the Committee for Open Science of the Ministry of Higher Education and Research.

 A partnership between the Committee for Open Science and Wikipedia funds Wikipedia residencies at higher education and research institutions to raise awareness among researchers and train them in science communication aligned with open science values.

France is pursuing a reform of research evaluation processes that extends beyond open science. This effort is coordinated through institutional adherence to the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA), with the French chapter of CoARA as the primary vehicle. Bringing evaluation processes into alignment with open science principles is one dimension of this broader reform.

 Specific clauses recognising open science practices have not yet been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. Research institutions and organisations are sovereign in evaluation matters. The report notes that 2025 is expected to see the publication of numerous CoARA action plans, which may address this gap; these are or will be available on Zenodo.

 The Committee for Open Science organises annual prizes recognising open science initiatives across all domains covered by the National Plan for Open Science.

 Unintended negative consequences of open science have been reported. Opposition has emerged in response to the dissemination of open science recommendations, documented and analysed in a recently published survey. The report notes the difficulty of identifying specific mitigation actions, as addressing these challenges is integral to ongoing open science policymaking.

France has not launched specific national or institutional initiatives promoting innovative participatory or procedural changes to increase openness across the research cycle. The report explains that such recommendations address research at its core, beyond the scope of the National Plan for Open Science, which focuses on opening research outputs. Issues of traceability and reproducibility are described as important and consistent with French policy, but the detailed analysis required has not been completed and will not be by 2025.


 One emerging measure is the policy on Data, Algorithms and Source Code, which aims  to modify and improve research processes through the integration of data management plans and entity data management plans.

France promotes international collaboration on open science through several initiatives it has created and finances. The Open Science Monitoring Initiative (OSMI), initiated by France and now operating internationally with UNESCO, aims to establish international principles for measuring progress in open science. The Council for National Open Science Coordination (CoNOSC), also financed by France, convenes a large proportion of European national open science coordinators to exchange practices. France contributed to the G7 Sendai communiqué of 2023 in support of open science, holds seats on the boards of ORCID (Open Researcher and Contributor ID) and Crossref, and finances the Global Research Initiative on Open Science (GRIOS), which federates existing evidence and academic research on open science.

 Cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is described as primarily European, conducted through CoNOSC.

 France participates in discussions on international funding mechanisms for open science through its engagement in the global federation on diamond publishing, including with UNESCO, to develop secure and sustainable funding models for diamond open access.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) were directly involved in preparing the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science, together with the Korean National Commission for UNESCO. Following its endorsement, MSIT, the Science and Technology Policy Institute (STEPI), the Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information (KISTI), the National Library of Korea, the National Assembly Library, the Korea Education and Research Information Service (KERIS) and other organisations have promoted its dissemination and implementation.

 

Awareness-raising activities have taken several forms. The Korean National Commission for UNESCO produced and distributed newsletters and videos about the Recommendation in 2021 and 2022. Since 2022, STEPI has organised regular domestic stakeholder meetings on implementation with MSIT support. The National Library of Korea holds an annual open access conference and repository workshops for policymakers, researchers, libraries and academic societies. KISTI raises awareness through its annual Future Research Information Forum and periodical AccessOn newsletters, and published a guidebook on open access publishing in 2021 and 2022.

 

These activities have generated collective commitments. A National Open Access Policy Forum in June 2021 led to a Joint Declaration on Open Access in August 2021, signed by six organisations including the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), KISTI and the Korean Federation of Science and Technology Societies (KOFST). In 2023, the National Library of Korea and KISTI launched a broader Open Access Declaration of Action, enabling additional parties to join; as of 2024, 23 organisations have signed.

The country's science, technology and innovation (STI) policy, the 5th Basic Plan on Science and Technology (2023-2027), includes open science elements such as an open research environment, digital research infrastructure and public engagement. The Framework Act on Science and Technology (official text in Korean), whose Article 5 requires governmental support for open innovation, affirms openness and collaboration. To promote the management and utilisation of research data at the national level, three bills proposing an Act on Promotion of National Research Data Management and Utilization were introduced in 2024.

 

Among specific policy instruments, MSIT devised the Strategy for Sharing and Utilizing Research Data in 2018, under which NRF, the research funding agency, began requiring data management plans for selected national research and development (R&D) projects. No single national open science strategy is reported, but the above-mentioned 5th Basic Plan on Science and Technology (2023–2027)and the 4th Basic Plan on the Management and Utilization of Research Outputs (2021-2025) support the development of open science-friendly environments; for example, the latter pursues an open research ecosystem in which outputs are accessible and well managed.

 

At the institutional level, nine institutions have open access policies registered in the Registry of Open Access Repository Mandates and Policies (ROARMAP), including the Korea Institute of Science and Technology Information (KISTI)and the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST).

 

No dedicated national funding mechanism for open science is reported, though article processing charges for open access publications can be reimbursed from national R&D project budgets. Korea's 2024 association with Pillar 2 of EU Horizon Europe is expected to require Korean participants to comply with its open access and data management requirements.

 

Regarding public-private partnerships on open science, KISTI organised a Transformative Agreement Working Group with relevant domestic stakeholders, following memoranda of understanding with the Korea University and College Library Association (KUCLA) and the Korea Special Library Association (KSLA) in 2021. Twenty-five government-funded research institutes under the National Research Council of Science and Technology (NST) signed agreements with Elsevier and Wiley in 2021, with additional agreements later expanded to other international publishers.

 

The report describes no overarching national monitoring framework for open science. KISTI operates Open Access Monitor Korea (publications) and DataOn Statistics (research data), while the Korea Journal Copyright Information (KJCI) system tracks domestic journal copyright policies and open access status.

The National Science and Technology Information Service (NTIS) has managed and disseminated R&D information since 2008. KISTI operates AccessOn, through which research institutions and academic societies can publish and use open access articles, and the national research data platform DataOn. KISTI also developed NaRDA, research data management software now deployed at 23 public institutions. The National Library of Korea's Open Access Korea (OAK) Repository Portal enables integrated search across institutional repositories. Field-specific platforms include the Korea Bio Data Station (K-BDS) and the Korea Materials Data Station (K-MDS). KISTI's ScienceOn delivers content through an Application Programming Interface (API) Gateway used by 253 organisations, and the Korea Digital Object Identifier (DOI) Centre has assigned approximately 26 million DOIs to publications and data from about 1,600 organisations.

 

Korea hosts and funds federated international information technology infrastructure. KISTI joined the Global Ring Network for Advanced Applications Development (GLORIAD) in 2005 and since 2023 has participated in the Global 100-Gbps Ring Research Network connecting Asia, North America and Europe. It operates networks dedicated to transferring Large Hadron Collider (LHC) experimental data from the European Organisation for Nuclear Research (CERN) and participates in the Sponsoring Consortium for Open Access Publishing in Particle Physics (SCOAP3). KISTI is also active in the Global Network Advancement Group (GNA-G) and the East Asia Resilient Backbone Network Collaboration (EARBN).

 

Several North-South and South-South collaborations are reported. KISTI provided consulting to customise the NTIS platform for Costa Rica and has partnered with the Kuwait Foundation for the Advancement of Science from 2022 to 2024 to organise Data-AI camps for Kuwaiti high school students. A representative from STEPI served as vice chair of the Science, Technology, Engineering and Innovation Policy Asia and the Pacific Network (STEPAN) from 2021 to 2024, promoting open science regionally.

Systematic capacity building on open science takes several forms. Departments of Library and Information Science at Korean universities have begun incorporating open access, research data management and open science into their curricula. The National Library of Korea organises regular workshops for OAK repository managers and provides educational resources to librarians and information service personnel through its education platform. KISTI operates the Scientific Data Education Centre, offering data literacy training, and the Korea Institute of Human Resources Development in Science and Technology (KIRD) provides training on research ethics and technological trends that includes open science content.

 

Regarding capacity building for policymakers, seminars and workshops were organised during the development of policies such as the Basic Plan on Science and Technology and the Strategy for Sharing and Utilizing Research Data. The report notes, however, that no long-standing educational programmes on open science exist specifically for policymakers.

 

The incorporation of open science competencies into higher education curricula, as reflected in the Library and Information Science programmes mentioned above, remains partial.

 

The report states that open educational resources are not used as an instrument for open science capacity building. Efforts to implement UNESCO's Recommendation on Open Educational Resources are ongoing but operate separately, as the Ministry of Education supports open educational resources while open science is primarily supported by MSIT.

 

In science communication, MSIT supports the Korea Foundation for the Advancement of Science and Creativity (KOSAC) in operating ScienceAll, a platform for disseminating scientific knowledge. Citizen science platforms such as Nature Ring operate in the field of biodiversity. The report notes, however, that no dedicated science communication project is exclusively focused on open science.

Regarding the review of research assessment processes, the 4th Basic Plan on National R&D Performance Evaluation (2021-2025) aims to address the limitations of dominant quantitative publication-based evaluation by placing greater emphasis on qualitative assessment of research outputs and research processes. The report notes, however, that no distinct evaluation mechanism grounded in open science principles exists in national R&D programmes.

 

Concerning open science practices in career evaluation, the 4th Basic Plan on National R&D Performance Evaluation recommends alternative indicators on social impacts for social problem-solving R&D. The report states, however, that recognising scientists' collaboration with societal members is not common in career development and promotion systems.

 

Two national-level initiatives recognise and reward open science practices. The Minister of Science and ICT annually confers the Knowledge Sharing Award upon five researchers for creating and sharing academic achievements via NTIS. The Minister of Culture, Sports and Tourism, through the National Library of Korea, has presented the Open Access Korea Merit Award since 2015 to individuals and organisations contributing to open access.

 

Regarding unintended negative consequences, the growth of open access journals has intensified problems of predatory journals and conferences. NRF established the Centre for Research Ethics (CRE) in 2020 to address this, and KISTI developed the Scholarly Ecosystem Against Fake Publishing Environment (SAFE) website to identify predatory journals.

Several initiatives promote innovative approaches at different stages of the research cycle. At the research agenda-setting stage, MSIT announced plans to improve national R&D projects through the open participation of domestic academic societies at the 2023 General Meeting of Academic Societies for R&D Innovation.

 

To increase openness in the use of research facilities, the government has since 2005 required that research facilities above a certain size purchased through national R&D projects be registered with the National Research Facilities and Equipment Centre (NFEC) and made publicly accessible so that researchers outside the original project team can identify and use them.

 

At the publication stage, KISTI developed the AccessON Peer Review Management System Plus (ACOMS+), which supports open peer review for domestic journals. When a submitted paper undergoes open peer review, its review information becomes available upon publication.

Korea promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through multilateral forums, institutional partnerships and global networks. The country has participated in the UNESCO Open Science Advisory Council from the early stage and continues to contribute to five UNESCO working groups on open science. Cooperation extends through the OECD, G20 and APEC. NRF collaborates with the Global Research Council (GRC), and KISTI has begun a joint research project with UNESCO headquarters (2024-2029) to advance global open science. Korean actors participate in regional networks such as the Confederation of Open Access Repositories (COAR) Asia and the Asia Pacific Advanced Network (APAN), as well as collaborate on research data with the Research Data Alliance (RDA), the Committee on Data (CODATA) and the World Data System (WDS). Korean institutions co-hosted International Data Week in 2022 with these three organisations.

 

Regarding a strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration, the report describes no plan specific to open science. National strategies for international STI cooperation, such as the Strategy for Global R&D Leading the World and the R&D Innovation Strategy (both 2023), indirectly support such collaborations by promoting global research partnerships and bringing research environments in line with global standards.

 

Korean experts participate in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science as part of the UNESCO Open Science Working Groups.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been shared with the Ministry of Economic Affairs, the Ministry of Health, Welfare and Sport, the Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations and the Ministry of Justice and Security. It has also been disseminated to national entities, including Open Science NL, the ten-year national initiative for open science embedded within the Dutch Research Council (Nederlandse Organisatie voor Wetenschappelijk Onderzoek, NWO), and the Netherlands National Commission for UNESCO.

 Awareness-raising activities span recurring national events and community engagement. The annual Open Science Festival, held since 2021, the annual Recognition and Rewards Festival , started the same year, and the annual Citizen Science Nederland National Networking Day, held since 2022, are among the main events. Open Science NL hosts community consultations through workshops and advisory panels, as set out in its community engagement strategy. Open Science Communities, bottom-up researcher initiatives present in each research university city, have received financial support from Open Science NL for their professional development.

 The Netherlands National Commission for UNESCO has undertaken targeted dissemination, publishing a brochure on the Recommendation in 2024 and distributing it to 220 international open science contacts and 43 national stakeholders. The Commission reflected on the Open Science NL work programme in cooperation with the UNESCO Chair in Diversity and Inclusion in Global Science at Leiden University. It also delivered a keynote on UNESCO's open science mission at the EuroScience Open Forum in 2022 and organised a workshop on artificial intelligence and open science in 2024.

Open science has been a national policy priority in the Netherlands since its 2016 EU Council Presidency, when the Amsterdam Call for Action on Open Science was launched. Several national policy letters, strategies and documents on science, technology and innovation have followed, including the 2022 Letter to Parliament on Higher Education and Science Policy,  which incorporates open science within broader science and research policy objectives.

 

The Taverne Amendment (Article 25fa of the Copyright Act, 2015) is a legal instrument that provides researchers with secondary publishing rights, allowing them to share publicly funded publications via repositories after a reasonable period, commonly implemented as six months, facilitating alongside institutional policies near-100% open access rates in many institutions.

 Open Science NL, established in 2023, is the principal national policy instrument, working along four programme lines: open scholarly communication, FAIR data, open research software and citizen science. The National Programme Open Science 2030 Ambition Document (2022) and the Open Science NL Work Programme 2024-2025 form the national implementation plan, with the 2021 Recommendation explicitly referenced. At institutional level, all 14 publicly funded research universities maintain open science policies, including Utrecht University and Radboud University Nijmegen.

 The Ministry of Education, Culture and Science provides €80 million for the Open Science NL programme (2023-2031) to fund a system-level transition towards open science. In addition, the NWO Open Science Fund, launched in 2020, awards grants for projects specifically designed to implement and stimulate open science practices. Open science principles are also embedded in funding requirements. For example, Plan S open access principles apply to all NWO and ZonMw (the national health research and innovation funder) calls since January 2021, and all grantees must make publications open access and data FAIR. NWO signed the San Francisco Declaration on Research Assessment (DORA) in 2019, banning journal impact factors and the h-index and introducing the evidence-based CV.

 Equitable public-private partnerships are pursued through transformative 'read and publish' agreements with scientific publishers. A 2024 conference explored the successes and challenges of these deals, informing a new agreement with Elsevier in 2025.

 Open Science NL has been tasked with monitoring open science, with €400,000 allocated for this purpose. Since 2016, Universities of the Netherlands (Universiteiten van Nederland, UNL) has published annual reports on open access (see the Open Access Monitor), tracking growth from 42% to 92% between 2016 and 2023.

National open science infrastructures span computing, storage and repository services. SURF, the national research and education network and IT cooperative, provides high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage. Data Archiving and Networked Services (DANS) is the national centre of expertise and repository for research data, and 4TU.ResearchData is a repository for data and software in science, engineering and design.

 Community-managed infrastructures and platforms for knowledge exchange are also in place. The National Library of the Netherlands (Koninklijke Bibliotheek, KB) and UKB, the consortium of the 13 Dutch university libraries, are researching the establishment of a public platform for scientific works. Citizen Science Nederland operates as a national community of practice for citizen science and knowledge platform, and the Citizen Science Lab at Leiden University supports co-creation between scientists and society. The eduSources platform provides open educational resources in higher education.

 Research information systems include the METIS Current Research Information System (CRIS), in use since the early 1990s, which administers information on research projects, personnel, organisations, funding and outputs.

The Netherlands reports bilateral memoranda of understanding with Brazil and South Africa that include open science as a topic for cooperation. Multilateral engagement involves the GO FAIR Virus Outbreak Data Network (VODAN), EU-Africa research cooperation and the Square Kilometre Array Observatory (SKAO). The UNESCO Chair in Diversity and Inclusion in Global Science at CWTS also contributes to this area.

Systematic capacity building on open science is supported through the Open Science NL Work Programme 2024-2025, which dedicates €22.4 million across four funding programmes: a national training platform for research data professionals, strengthening local and thematic Digital Competence Centres, Citizen Science Hubs and strengthening institutional non-profit open access publishing.

The report does not describe capacity building programmes dedicated specifically to policymakers. It notes that institutions build capacity through networks and communities such as library working groups, research data management networks, Open Science Communities and Citizen Science Nederland.

 The report indicates that a national framework of open science competencies has not been established, as the Netherlands does not have a national curriculum. At institutional level, most universities offer open science courses at PhD and research master's level, including at Erasmus University Rotterdam and TU Delft.

 Open educational resources are used for open science capacity building through eduSources, the Npuls National Growth Fund programme and the Acceleration Plan Educational Innovation with ICT.

 Science communication in line with open science principles is supported by the National Expert Centre for Science and Society (Nationaal Expertisecentrum Wetenschap en Samenleving, NEWS), established in 2024.

Initiatives to review research assessment centre on the national Recognition and Rewards Programme, launched in 2019 by universities, university medical centres, research institutes and research funders. One of its five ambitions is to stimulate all aspects of open science in assessment and evaluation of academics. In 2023, participating institutions set the goal of clarifying how open science activities will be considered in the development, assessment, appointment and promotion of staff. The programme is co-funded by the Ministry of Education, Culture and Science (2022-2026). In December 2024, Open Science NL awarded €1.14 million to 24 research institutions to embed open science in recruitment and promotion criteria, with an additional €150,000 for national coordination.

 Specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation at several institutions. At Maastricht University, impact and open science form a mandatory part of assessment for senior academics. At VU Amsterdam, open science is conditional to performance assessments and promotion. The promotion profile for “Professor 1” at Utrecht University requires the holder to actively promote open science.

 Initiatives to recognise and reward open science include the Leo Waaijers Open Science Award and the VU Open Science Challenge Award. Research Data Netherlands (RDNL) awards the biennial Dutch Data Prize for FAIR research data in three categories.

 The report states that no unintended negative consequences of open science practices have been reported. It notes, however, that the international shift from 'pay to read' to 'pay to publish' business models and rising article processing charges may constitute an example, with the impact on the Global South being examined by the UNESCO Chair in Diversity and Inclusion in Global Science at the Centre for Science and Technology Studies.

Participatory and innovative approaches are promoted at both national and institutional levels. The Dutch Research Agenda (Nederlandse Wetenschapsagenda, NWA) places citizens at the centre of creating research priorities. Governments, researchers, civil-society organisations and citizens work together to develop and use knowledge for scientific and societal impact. The Agenda also incorporates strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities. Citizen Science Nederland operates as the national network for participatory research, and Open Science NL funds Citizen Science Hubs at institutional level to build regional expertise in societal engagement.

 Preprints are addressed ad hoc at institutional level, with several institutions supporting infrastructure such as ArXiv. The report notes that no national policy or mandate for preprints exists, though this is being discussed. The valuing and sharing of negative scientific results is addressed through community initiatives, including the Journal of Trial and Error, which originated in the Netherlands.

The NWO provides financial support for international open science infrastructures. Several university libraries have established dedicated funds for this purpose, such as at Utrecht University. Coordination and development of collective funding mechanisms are reported to be underway.

 The Netherlands pursues cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration through several channels, as affirmed in multiple letters to Parliament. Engagement occurs through multilateral organisations including the EU, OECD and G20. In 2024, the Netherlands donated €100,000 to UNESCO for the implementation of the Recommendation on Open Science. Bilateral memoranda of understanding with countries in the Global North and Global South include open science as a topic for cooperation.

 Regarding regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, The NWO financially supports the Global Research Initiative on Open Science (GRIOS), which reviews existing evidence and ongoing research on open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Education and Research and the Research Council of Norway (RCN). For awareness raising, the Norwegian Agency for Shared Services in Education and Research (Sikt) has established a website maintained jointly with the RCN that provides an updated overview of national and international open science activities.

Norway has several national policies and strategic documents related to or specifically mentioning open science. Among them the White Paper No. 5 to the Norwegian Parliament (Meld. St. 5 (2022–2023)), which promotes the accessibility of research-based knowledge, including through open access to scientific publications, the RCN's Policy on Open Science, and the Strategy for Scientific Publishing after 2024 (Strategi for vitenskapelig publisering etter 2024).

 

The report identifies several policy instruments underpinning Norway’s implementation of open science, including the Norwegian scheme for open journals in the social sciences and humanities (NÅHST), the National Open Archive (Nasjonalt vitenarkiv), the Norwegian Register of Scientific Publishing Channels acting as a register of recommended publishing venues and a gatekeeper against unreliable publishers, and nationally coordinated publish and read agreements.

 

At institutional level, all Norwegian higher education institutions have open science strategies. The Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU) has the Policy for Open Science covering research, artistic research, teaching and dissemination. The University of Bergen (UiB) has a policy addressing open access to publications, research data, open innovation, open educational resources and citizen science, while the University of Oslo (UiO) has maintained an open access policy for over a decade.

 

The report does not identify a specific national funding mechanism for open science, though nationally coordinated publish and read agreements are managed by Sikt. Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding through the RCN's assessment of open science practices as part of the overall grant evaluation.

 

The report does not describe efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science.

 

Open access to research publications is monitored and published annually as part of the national monitoring framework for open science.

Norway's national open science infrastructures encompass federated information technology infrastructure, including high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage, as well as community-managed infrastructures, protocols and standards. Sigma2 AS provides the national e-infrastructure for computational science with high-performance computing and large-scale data storage for research and educational purposes. The Norwegian Node of the Research Data Alliance (RDA) supports Norwegian researchers, institutions and organisations in research data management and sharing.

 

The report does not describe Norway’s involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms.

Systematic capacity building on open science takes place across most Norwegian higher education institutions, with UiO, UiB, the University of Tromsø – The Arctic University of Norway (UiT) and NTNU, all offering courses in research data management and open science. The Norwegian Directorate for Higher Education and Skills (HK-dir) participates in the Skills for EOSC project.

 

The report does not describe capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies.

 

Open educational resources are reported as partly used for open science capacity building. The report provides no further detail.

 

Science communication initiatives are pursued across most higher education institutions. The 2024 conference of Universities Norway (Universitets- og høgskolerådet) focused on open research, open education and open society.

Initiatives to align research assessment and evaluation processes with open science principles include the adoption by many higher education institutions of the Agreement on Reforming Research Assessment. NOR-CAM, a Norwegian toolbox for recognition and rewards in academic careers, is a national chapter in the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA), and many institutions are adapting the NOR-CAM framework into their assessment frameworks for hiring and promoting researchers.

 

Specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems through institutional adaptations of NOR-CAM. The University of Oslo has developed a guide for members of expert committees, Oslo University Hospital has created its own adaptation (OUS-CAM) and the University of Stavanger has introduced a competence assessment matrix for teaching and research positions (UiS-KVM).

 

Initiatives to recognise and reward open science include the RCN's assessment of open science practices as part of its grant evaluation. Applicants may include practices such as early open sharing of research results in their CVs for RCN grant applications.

 

Regarding unintended negative consequences of open science, the report notes that these relate mainly to predatory journals reported to the Norwegian Register of Scientific Publishing Channels.

Initiatives promoting innovative approaches at different stages of the research cycle operate at both national and institutional levels, covering preprints, open peer review, valuing negative scientific results, citizen science and open innovation.

 

In the area of preprints and open peer review, the RCN supports the expansion of the Open Research Europe platform from 2026. In relation to negative results, Foundation Dam (Stiftelsen Dam), a foundation funding health research, requires all trials and studies it finances to preregister in its registry at the Open Science Framework (OSF). The RCN coordinates a national network for citizen science research (Nasjonalt nettverk for folkeforsking) and has participated in the European PRO-Ethics project, which developed ethical guidelines for participatory processes in the activities of research funding organisations.

The RCN promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through active participation in cOAlition S, the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA), Science Europe and the Global Research Council (GRC).

 

Norway's strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, including exchange of best practices, operates through Science Europe and the GRC.

 

Norway does not report involvement at the national level in discussions on establishing regional or international funding mechanisms for open science. As a member of cOAlition S and Science Europe, however, the RCN engages in such discussions, for example in relation to diamond open access publishing through the DIAMAS (Developing Institutional Open Access Publishing Models to Advance Scholarly Communication) project.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted by several national institutions, such as the Ministry of Education and Sport (MoES), the National Agency for Scientific Research and Innovation (NASRI), the Academy of Sciences of Albania (ASA), the Agency for Innovation and Excellence (AIE) and Higher Education Institutions (HIE). These institutions foster an open science environment at the national level.

 

Awareness-raising activities have addressed multiple elements of open science. The MoES and NASRI organised several open science round tables, seminars and training sessions for researchers and policymakers. Reports and discussions led by the ASA have focused on the benefits of open access publications and open educational resources. The AIE hosted workshops on integrating open-source software, code and open hardware in start-ups and research institutions, while HEIs run seminars for students and faculty, focusing on open access publishing, open educational resources and engagement with societal actors.

Albania's science, technology and innovation policy includes open science elements in two principal instruments, which constitute the national roadmap for open science implementation. The National Strategy for Scientific Research, Technology, and Innovation 2023–2030, aligned with EU frameworks, such as Horizon Europe, outlines a range of measures to ensure that scientific knowledge is openly shared and accessible to the public. Law No. 109/2024 “On Science and Scientific Research” provides the legal foundation: Article 24 explicitly obliges researchers to respect open science principles, and Article 5(6) mandates the adoption of standards through a decision of the Council of Ministers to ensure the implementation of this law and its open science provisions in alignment with European Research Area (ERA) policies.

At institutional level, several HEIs have adopted open science policies (focusing on making research data and findings publicly available) and designed action plans, aligning their practices with Horizon Europe requirements.

 

NASRI is a government agency that manages public research funding through national research and development projects and bilateral and multilateral cooperation. Open science practices are integrated into these funding mechanisms: researchers receiving NASRI grants are required to make their publications and data openly available, and Horizon Europe mandates similarly apply to Albanian participants.

 

NASRI funding calls encourage public-private partnerships between research institutions and the private sector, requiring research outcomes to be openly shared. For example, the Durana Technology Park  brings together researchers, entrepreneurs and businesses for collaborative open science projects.

 

The report states that no dedicated national monitoring framework for open science exists. The National Strategy 2023–2030 includes science, technology and innovation indicators that may indirectly support the monitoring of open science practices (e.g., open access publications and institutional partnerships fostering open innovation), and the MoES plans to design a dedicated open science monitoring framework in collaboration with NASRI, the ASA, the Albanian Academic Network (AAN) and HEIs.

National open science infrastructures span federated information technology systems, community-managed platforms and platforms for knowledge exchange. The Centre for Coordination and Dissemination of Scientific Information (QKPISH) within the ASA operates COBISS.AL and E-CRIS.AL as interlinked bibliographic and research information systems serving libraries and research institutions nationwide. The dCOBISS digital repository preserves and provides open access to scientific publications from academic and research institutions. The ACRIS platform, under development by the MoES, NASRI and NAIS, will function as a federated national database for all research outputs.

 

Albania participates in federated regional and international open science infrastructures. Through the National Initiatives for Open Science in Europe (NI4OS-Europe) project, the country established the Albanian Initiative for Open Science (AI4OS) coalition, which promotes open science policies in HEIs and research infrastructures and coordinates integration of national services into the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC). AI4OS aims to establish a national cloud for open science within the EOSC ecosystem.

 

International collaborations to optimise infrastructure use include Albania's association with Horizon Europe, enabling cross-border research partnerships. The Regional Cooperation Council-led Open Access Research Infrastructure in the Western Balkans Support Programme aims to enhance regional cooperation.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in HEIs and research institutions through both formal and informal channels. Albanian universities, in collaboration with national and international partners, have organised workshops and seminars on open access, open data, open educational resources and the use of open science platforms. Albania's participation in Horizon Europe has prompted further training on data management and dissemination. The ASA, through the QKPISH, coordinates capacity-building initiatives on open access within research institutions.

 

Capacity-building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science principles into science, technology and innovation policies are addressed through Law No. 109/2024, which mandates the integration of open science into national policies, and the National Strategy for Scientific Research, Technology, and Innovation 2023–2030, which outlines targeted capacity-building efforts. The report provides no further detail.

 

A framework of open science competencies is not reported as fully incorporated into higher education curricula. Some HEIs include open data sharing, open access publishing and other open science practices in research methodology courses, and participation in EU-funded projects provides further opportunities for competency development.

 

The report indicates that open educational resources are used as an instrument for open science capacity building, though no specific examples are provided.

 

Science communication initiatives support the dissemination of scientific knowledge to researchers, policymakers and the public. The ASA publishes scientific journals and periodicals accessible through COBISS.AL and dCOBISS, and organises conferences, workshops and public talks to facilitate dialogue between scientists, policymakers and the public. NASRI supports dissemination through events and its website. HEIs promote scientific literacy through outreach activities, including public lectures, videos and science fairs.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes are under discussion at both national and institutional levels. The National Strategy for Scientific Research, Technology, and Innovation 2023–2030 acknowledges the need to align these processes with open science principles, including transparency of research outputs, data and methodologies. HEIs are discussing the inclusion of open science criteria alongside traditional metrics such as journal impact factor, citations and h-index, and involvement in Horizon Europe has stimulated these discussions.

 

Specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems are not yet formally in place, according to the report. Career evaluations at research institutions currently focus on traditional indicators such as published papers, citations and funding. The National Strategy 2023–2030 and Law No. 109/2024 lay the groundwork for introducing such changes.

 

There have been efforts to recognise and reward open science practices, with further initiatives planned under Law No. 109/2024, including a Council of Ministers' decision to set standards and criteria for open science implementation. Some HEIs are beginning to consider open science practices in their evaluation criteria for research output. The ASA awards  merit-based scientific prizes, including the “Aleksa Buda Prize”, the “Pilika Scientific Prize”, the “Eqrem Çabej Prize” and the “Petrit Radovicka Prize”. However, the information provided in the report does not clarify whether open science is integrated into these prizes.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

Initiatives promoting innovative approaches to increase the openness of the scientific process operate at both national and institutional levels. The reported areas of activity include open peer review, participatory methods such as citizen science and crowdsourcing, participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities, and open innovation. For example, HEIs, the ASA and research centres are exploring collaborative open science platforms that facilitate a participatory research cycle, enabling researchers to share work, receive feedback and engage with the public.

International scientific collaboration on open science is promoted through several channels. The National Strategy for Science, Technology, and Innovation 2023–2030 prioritises international cooperation, and Albania's association with Horizon Europe enables researchers to participate in cross-border projects emphasising open science. The Durana Technology Park encourages international partnerships for open innovation.

 

Albania's strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration is shaped by its EU integration process and regional participation. Through the Regional Cooperation Council, Albania engages in collaborative research with universities, research centres and industry representatives across the Western Balkans, including exchange of best practices and capacity building on open science. The NanoAlb Centre coordinates research groups from Albania, Kosovo, North Macedonia, Serbia, Spain, Greece, Israel and Italy. Its NanoAlb Ignite project promotes collaboration and knowledge exchange across nanoscience and other fields.

 

Albania participates in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science. Through Horizon Europe, the country contributes to and benefits from discussions on funding mechanisms encouraging open access, data sharing and collaborative research. Regional discussions within the Western Balkans address co-funding of collaborative projects and the establishment of open science funding mechanisms accessible across the region.

The report indicates that the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been shared with national entities responsible for the development of cooperation and partnership. Awareness-raising events are planned to promote the culture of open science.

The report indicates that a national science, technology and innovation policy includes open science elements: open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure, open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems. The policy is referenced in connection with the Directorate-General for Scientific Research and Technological Development (DGRSDT). 
 
At the national level, the policy and legal framework addressing open science is generally described as partial. Specific policy instruments are under development; for example, commissions have been established to generalise the use of open-source software and to strengthen international partnerships. 
 
Institutional-level policies promoting open science are similarly reported as under development. 
 
 Specific national funding mechanisms for open science and monitoring framework are under development and described as under exploration. The report states that open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms; however, the reference provided (related to inter-university cooperation activities) does not clarify whether open science requirements are explicitly attached to funding mechanisms such as research grants, with no further details provided.  
 
Regarding equitable public-private partnerships, the report states that partnership avenues are under discussion and it has not clarified whether open science is explicitly integrated into these partnerships.

National open science infrastructures are reported as under development, covering hree types: federated information technology infrastructure encompassing high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage; platforms for exchanges and co-creation of knowledge between scientists and society; and community-based monitoring and information systems. The report also points to the digital platforms being developed by the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research. 

 

Existing open science infrastructures at regional and international levels are reported as not accessible to the country's open science actors. 

 

Regarding North-South and South-South collaborations, Algeria reports involvement through international cooperation programmes, though no further details are provided.

Capacity building on open science in higher education and research-performing institutions has taken the form of organising and participating in scientific and technical meetings. 

 

The report affirms the use of open educational resources for open science capacity building but provides no further details. 

 

Across the areas of policymaker capacity building on open science, integration of open science competencies into higher education curricula, and science communication in line with open science, the report describes the relevant measures as under exploration.

Across the areas of research assessment and evaluation, career evaluation and progression, and recognition and reward of open science practices, the report describes relevant initiatives as being under exploration, with no details provided.
The report states that no specific initiatives promoting innovative, participatory changes at different stages of the research cycle have been undertaken. This area is described as under exploration.

Across the areas of international scientific collaboration on open science, cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration, and regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, the report describes relevant measures as under exploration.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through Government Offices and shared with relevant institutions and organisations.

 Awareness-raising activities on open science have taken place between 2021 and 2024. In 2021, the Association of Swedish Higher Education Institutions (SUHF) referenced the Recommendation in its Roadmap for Open Science. In March 2022, the Swedish Research Council and the National Library of Sweden each referenced the Recommendation in reports on their respective government mandates to coordinate open access to research data and scholarly publications. That same month, the Swedish National Commission for UNESCO and Public & Science Sweden (Vetenskap & Allmänhet), an independent non-profit organisation promoting dialogue between researchers and the public, convened a meeting to discuss the Recommendation with actors working on open science. In May 2023, the National Library of Sweden, the Swedish Research Council and Public & Science Sweden jointly organised the conference "Open Science – from Policy to Practice", with a UNESCO representative participating. A further conference in May 2024, organised by the National Library of Sweden and the Swedish Research Council, presented the National Guidelines for Open Science, with a UNESCO representative participating.

Sweden's science, technology and innovation policy takes the form of a research and innovation bill presented every four years. The 2024 bill addressed open access to research data and scholarly publications and the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA).

 The above-mentioned National Guidelines for Open Science, adopted in 2024 by the National Library of Sweden with the Swedish Research Council, serve as both a national policy framework and implementation roadmap. They are structured around open access to publications and research data, open research methods, open educational resources, public engagement in science and supporting infrastructures. SUHF has developed a complementary Roadmap for Open Science with implementation guidance.

 Specific policy instruments promoting open science include government mandates to the National Library of Sweden and Swedish Research Council to coordinate open access to scholarly publications and research data. The Bibsam Consortium, some 95 institutions coordinated by the National Library of Sweden, uses transformative agreements to shift from subscriptions to open access.

 

At institutional level, the National Library of Sweden has made an inventory of open science policies at higher education institutions. The Swedish Research Council's follow-up (2024) indicates several organisations have adopted policies for open access to research data.

 As a national funding mechanism, four research public funders – the Swedish Research Council, the Swedish Research Council for Health, Working Life and Welfare (Forte), the Swedish Research Council for Sustainable Development (Formas) and Sweden's Innovation Agency (Vinnova) – agreed in 2023 to jointly fund publications with fully open access publishers for 2024–2025. Open science practices are integrated into existing funding: the Swedish Research Council requires data management plans since 2019 and open access for all publications since October 2022.

 No efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported.

 National monitoring covers specific open science components; no overall monitoring framework is reported. The National Library of Sweden has tracked open access to scholarly publications through "Open access in numbers" since 2017. The Swedish Research Council reports annually on open access to research data. SUHF conducts an annual survey tied to its Roadmap for Open Science.

Sweden reports the availability of national open science infrastructures primarily in two main categories: federated information technology infrastructure and community-based monitoring and information systems. Specifically, the Swedish Research Council funds a range of research infrastructures, several relevant to open science. The Swedish National Data Service (SND), funded by the Swedish Research Council, supports accessibility, preservation and reuse of research data through a network of around 40 universities and public research institutes. Swepub serves as a national portal for Swedish scholarly publications, while Publicera is a national platform for scholarly open access journals; both are maintained by the National Library of Sweden. The Digital Scientific Archive (Digitala Vetenskapliga Arkivet, DiVA) is an institutional repository used by 50 higher education and research institutions. Medborgarforskning.se is a national hub for citizen science. SciLifeLab provides a national digital infrastructure and services for life scientists.

 Sweden hosts and funds several federated regional and international infrastructures, including the National Academic Infrastructure for Supercomputing in Sweden (NAISS), the European Spallation Source, the Integrated Carbon Observation System (ICOS) Carbon Portal, the Swedish node of the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) and the Human Protein Atlas. The Swedish University Computer Network (Sunet) connects higher education and research institutions nationally and globally.

 The report does not provide information on Sweden’s involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations on open science infrastructure.

Systematic capacity building on open science in higher education and research institutions is pursued through the SUHF Roadmap for Open Science, which clarifies the responsibilities and actions needed by higher education institutions to transition to an open science system. Formalised through decisions by the SUHF general assembly, the Roadmap is continuously monitored and occasionally revised. Individual organisations also undertake both formal and informal capacity building activities, sometimes cooperatively.

 The report does not describe capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies.

 No framework of open science competencies has been reported as incorporated into higher education research skills curricula. Eighteen higher education institutions are, however, working with the Human Resources Strategy for Researchers (HRS4R), a European Commission initiative that includes open science. PhD courses on open science have been developed at several institutions, including Stockholm University, Linköping University and Umeå University.

 Open educational resources are partly used for open science capacity building, though the report provides no further detail.

 In the area of science communication, the Swedish Research Council is mandated by the government to coordinate science communication and encourage dialogue between researchers and other societal actors. It coordinates forskning.se, a website that communicates research from Swedish universities, research institutes and research organisations to a wider audience. Public & Science Sweden, an independent non-profit, promotes dialogue and openness between researchers and the public.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes include SUHF's working group on merits and evaluation. The Swedish Research Council has adopted a position on researcher merit assessment that references research data.

 To recognise and reward open science practices, several Swedish institutions have signed the CoARA Agreement on Reforming Research Assessment, and a CoARA National Chapter Sweden has been established. Science communication awards are given by several bodies, including Örebro University and the Hamrin Foundation, the Young Academy of Sweden, the Forum for Science Communication and the Centre for Business and Policy Studies (SNS); however, the information provided in the report has not clarified whether open science is explicitly integrated into these awards.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

Reported initiatives focus on participatory methods that value inputs from societal actors, particularly citizen science. In 2023, the National Library of Sweden completed a government-commissioned survey and analysis of public participation in the scientific process and issued recommendations to promote such participation. The SUHF Roadmap for Open Science also addresses aspects of openness throughout the research process. Medborgarforskning.se collects citizen science projects in Sweden and is connected to the EU citizen science initiative.

Sweden promotes international collaboration on open science primarily through participation in European and international networks. At the European level, Sweden is active in the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC), the Open Access Infrastructure for Research in Europe (OpenAIRE), Open Research Europe (ORE) and the Council for National Open Science Coordination (CoNOSC), among others. The National Library of Sweden and higher education institutions also support international open science services, including the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ), the Open Researcher and Contributor ID (ORCID), the Scholarly Publishing and Academic Resources Coalition (SPARC) Europe and arXiv.org.

 Regarding cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration, the Swedish Research Council is mandated by the government to coordinate Swedish engagement in EOSC. National strategies addressing this area include the Swedish strategy for participation in Horizon Europe 2021–2027, the Lund Declaration on Maximising the Benefits of Research Data and the Swedish Position Paper on FP10, the next EU Research and Innovation Framework Programme.

 On regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, several Swedish public research funding organisations are involved in discussions on financing the Open Research Europe (ORE) platform.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted and shared with relevant ministries, institutions and affiliated organisations in Angola. Government entities involved include the Ministry of Education, the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Technology and Innovation (MESCTI), the Foundation for Scientific and Technological Development (FUNDECIT) and the National Commission for UNESCO in Angola. International partners such as UNESCO and Redalyc/AmeliCA have supported these efforts, and multiple universities have participated, among them Oscar Ribas University, Agostinho Neto University and the University of Katyavala Bwila. 

 

Awareness-raising activities have been organised, and further activities are planned through the end of 2025. Workshops and seminars, delivered in collaboration with UNESCO, Redalyc/AmeliCA and MESCTI, have focused on diamond open access, scientific publishing and research visibility, while a Conference on Open Science was held within the framework of the Community of Portuguese Language Countries (Comunidade dos Países de Língua Portuguesa, CPLP). Training programmes at Oscar Ribas University and other academic institutions have addressed open science principles, research data management and journal indexing. National discussions on integrating open science into higher education and research policies have also taken place.

Angola's science, technology and innovation (STI) policy, the National Policy on Science, Technology and Innovation, was adopted in 2011. According to the report, it does not yet adequently incorporate open science elements. 

 

The principal national legal framework addressing open science is Joint Executive Decree No. 303/22 , which regulates funding granted by FUNDECIT for scientific research and development. Its Article 18 requires that results from FUNDECIT-funded projects be published and made accessible in indexed open access journals. No additional specific policy instruments to promote open science are reported. 

 

A national open science implementation plan is under development. Ministerial Dispatch No. 252/24, issued in October 2024, established a working group of university experts to develop a National Strategy on Open Access. Angola also reported plans to develop a broader national formal policy on open science. Institutional policies promoting open science are likewise reported as under development. 

 

Specific national funding mechanisms for open science are under development, with no dedicated budget yet allocated. Decree No. 303/22 partially integrates open science practices into existing research funding by requiring open access publication as a condition of FUNDECIT grants. 

 

The creation of the Angolan Open Access Repository (Repositório Angolano de Acesso Aberto, RAnAA) is reported as a collaborative initiative exemplifying equitable public-private partnerships, which involved MESCTI, Expertise France, the European Union, UNESCO, Oscar Ribas University and Redalyc-AmeliCA, among others. 

 

A national monitoring framework for open science is under development, with MESCTI working to incorporate open science indicators into its STI policy assessment.

The main national open science infrastructure is the Angolan Open Access Repository (RAnAA), a non-commercial platform for collecting, preserving and disseminating scientific outputs from Angolan researchers. RAnAA adheres to the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) principles and is governed through a community-based approach involving multiple stakeholders. It primarily functions as a community-managed infrastructure supporting bibliodiversity and societal engagement by providing open access to Angolan scientific publications.  

 

Angola does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Existing regional and international open science infrastructures are, however, reported as accessible to relevant Angolan actors. 

 

Angola is involved in North-South and South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use and develop shared open science platforms. As a member of CPLP, Angola participates in the community's open access repository initiative for scientific publications. Since 2020, MESCTI and Oscar Ribas University have collaborated with the Autonomous University of the State of Mexico through the Redalyc-AmeliCA to improve the visibility of Angolan scientific journals and train researchers.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research-performing institutions through formal initiatives. In 2020, UNESCO partnered with MESCTI to organise webinars on creating, managing and indexing open access scientific journals. Open Science Advocacy Workshops were conducted in 2023 to increase awareness of the benefits of open science and support its adoption within African higher education institutions. 

 

Capacity building programmes for policymakers have also taken place. The 2020 webinars facilitated discussions on developing a national open access policy and on long-term cooperation between UNESCO and the Government of Angola towards nationwide open access. 

 

No framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report. This is attributed to constrained state budgets and scarce private financing limiting investment in scientific research. The Tertiary Education, Science, and Technology Project (TEST), launched in December 2023 with World Bank support as a ten-year programme to improve the quality of higher education, is described as laying groundwork for future integration of open science competencies. 

 

The report indicates that open educational resources are used as an instrument for open science capacity building but does not provide further detail. 

 

Initiatives to support science communication in line with open science principles have focused on disseminating scientific knowledge and information to researchers, decision-makers and the public. The 2020 UNESCO-MESCTI webinars addressed strategies for improving access to scientific information and for establishing a network of scientific journals in Angola.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes have been undertaken at the national level. The 2020 webinars organised by UNESCO and MESCTI discussed mechanisms for accrediting national scientific journals and publishers, assessed training needs for editorial teams and established criteria for indexing journals produced in Angola. A 2022 study titled "Open access in Angola: a survey among higher education institutions" found moderate awareness of open access among Angolan higher education institutions but slow practical incorporation into academic processes, including research assessment. 

 

The report states that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. Cited obstacles include the absence of a national policy mandating such integration, limited institutional adoption of open science in evaluation frameworks and the need for further training and guidelines. 

 

No formal national initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported. The report notes that universities have not implemented structured policies to incentivise open science, that researchers continue to prioritise traditional academic outputs and that no government-led or institutional awards for open science exist. 

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.
