For example, Aarhus University has established an Open Science Committee, consisting of a dean, all the vice deans of research and the vice director of research support, to develop all university open science policies; the University of Southern Denmark revised its Open Science Policy in 2024, expanding it to cover a broader range of elements, including FAIR data, open access, the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA) and citizen science.

Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding: the Danish National Research Foundation has introduced FAIR-related requirements for grant recipients and approves dedicated data managers, while Innovation Fund Denmark requires data management plans for some instruments and funds capped open access publishing costs for grant recipients.

 Regarding equitable public-private partnerships on open science, five universities operate the Open Discovery Innovation Network (ODIN), which fosters open, patent-free collaboration between industry and academia to co-create research projects relevant for drug discovery and diagnostics.

 

The Ministry of Higher Education and Science operates an Open Access Indicator, which tracks annual open access progress and serves as the national monitoring framework for the implementation of the Danmark’s Strategy on Open Access.

DeiC Dataverse is a national digital repository where researchers from Danish institutions can publish research data; linked services for data storage and sensitive data storage are under development. DeiC hosts a national DataCite consortium providing persistent identifier infrastructure that connects research outputs and resources.

 

Denmark participates in European Research Infrastructure Consortia (ERICs), the European Strategy Forum on Research Infrastructures (ESFRI) and the Global Dataverse Community Consortium (GDCC). Through the EOSC EU Node, Denmark accesses community-based information systems while developing national equivalents; DeiC is a mandated member of the EOSC Association.

 

Several Open Journal Systems (OJS) platforms provide open access publishing without article processing charges (APCs), including Tidsskrift.dk for Danish scientific journals, the Copenhagen Business School platform Rauli and Aalborg University's diamond publishing platform AAU OPEN.

 

Denmark contributes to federated international open science infrastructure by developing the EOSC through its National Strategy for Data Management Based on the FAIR Principles. DeiC is exploring the possibility of establishing a Danish or Nordic EOSC node to federate with the EOSC EU Node and other national nodes.

 

Denmark's membership in the EU and in international research infrastructures, including CERN (European Organization for Nuclear Research), the European Southern Observatory (ESO), the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL), the European Synchrotron Radiation Facility (ESRF), the European X-Ray Free-Electron Laser (European XFEL) and the European Spallation Source (ESS), enables collaborations with stakeholders in Latin America, Africa and Asia that promote open science and open access to FAIR data.

Systematic capacity building on open science has focused on ensuring national infrastructure, primarily DeiC Dataverse, and on increasing the uptake of persistent identifiers for researchers, data and instruments. Danish universities support open science capacity building with varying levels of resources. In 2025, Aalborg University is launching Open Science Community Aalborg as part of the Skills4EOSC initiative, and Copenhagen Business School leads Open Science Community Copenhagen.
 
A national framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, though the report notes that some universities already incorporate such competencies locally to varying degrees. For example, at the Technical University of Denmark (DTU), research data management, the FAIR principles and data publication are integrated into mandatory Responsible Conduct of Research training for all PhD students. National discussions on the development of a competency framework are under way, led by DeiC's board and informed by the National Strategy for Data Management Based on the FAIR Principles, the EOSC Strategic Research and Innovation Agenda and its Multi-Annual Roadmap and the Skills4EOSC project.
 
The Danish Science Festival, an annual week-long event held in April, supports science communication by creating meeting points between researchers and citizens. It comprises more than 700 lectures and events across the country and attracts around 70,000 visitors each year.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation include engagement of universities and research foundations with the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA) by signing the Agreement on Reforming Research Assessment (ARRA). For example, Aalborg University developed an institutional research and innovation indicator in 2022 that is compliant with ARRA and works with open science through a focus on collaboration, visibility and openness. Independent Research Fund Denmark launched its Fund Strategy 2024–2026 and a CoARA Action Plan for 2024–2027, which aims to develop new assessment criteria that give less weight to high-profile journal publications, establish a Danish CoARA network and introduce a new CV format recognising diverse roles and career paths.

 

Universities Denmark's framework for the recognition of knowledge dissemination provides guidance on how open science practices can be recognised in career contexts. The ongoing update of the Danish Code of Conduct for Research Integrity will further embed open science practices within national guidelines for responsible research, covering FAIR data management, open access and citizen science.

 

The report identifies rising publication costs as an unintended negative consequence of open science practices. While licence expenditure has remained relatively stable, costs of open access publishing with APCs, primarily in gold open access journals, have risen significantly. According to the evaluation of Denmark's National Strategy for Open Access, this increase cannot be attributed solely to higher publication volume.

The report identifies initiatives at the institutional level that promote innovative approaches at different stages of the research cycle. For example, the University of Southern Denmark has established a Citizen Science Knowledge Center, a hub for open participatory practices that encompasses all five faculties and the Odense University Hospital. The above-mentioned Aalborg University's research and innovation indicator values diversity in research outputs, including preprints, and awards innovation in research processes through annual strategic performance agreements between departments and faculties.

Denmark is actively engaged in EU open science initiatives alongside other EU Member States and Associated Countries (e.g., the Knowledge Exchange partnership on developing open science infrastructures and services). Denmark has endorsed the EU Council Conclusions on Open Science (2016) and the Council Conclusions on the European Open Science Cloud (2018). The report identifies this EU-level engagement as Denmark's approach to cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science.

 

Denmark does not report involvement in discussions on establishing regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Strengthening Research Management and Open Science Capacities of Higher Education Institutions in Moldova and Armenia (MINERVA) Erasmus+ programme, coordinated by the Ministry of Education, Science, Culture and Sports. Four universities participated: the Armenian State University of Economics, Yerevan Brusov State University of Languages and Social Sciences, Yerevan State Medical University and Yerevan State University. Representatives of other research organisations and universities also attended informational meetings and trainings under the programme.

The Roadmap for Integration of Armenia's Research and Development into the European Research Area (Ministry Order No. 59, January 2020) addresses activities on open access to scientific publications and data and on knowledge transfer. No dedicated national policy or implementation plan for open science is currently in place. However, an Expert Group for Open Science was established by ministerial order of the Minister of Education, Science, Culture and Sports in December 2021 to study the possibilities of full open science implementation in Armenia, and the Ministry has elaborated a draft "Goals and Guidelines on Open Science Policy of the Republic of Armenia", whose adoption is expected to follow enactment of a new Law on Higher Education and Science. 
 
At the institutional level, four universities developed open science strategies for 2021–2026 under the MINERVA programme: Yerevan State University, the Armenian State University of Economics, Yerevan Brusov State University of Languages and Social Sciences and Yerevan State Medical University. 
No specific national funding mechanism for open science is reported, though open science activities are supported within three funding streams: baseline funding (e.g., research facilities and equipment), grant programme funding and targeted interdepartmental cooperation funding. In 2024, the Higher Education and Science Committee of the Ministry of Education, Science, Culture and Sport launched a grant programme of approximately one million euros to reimburse publication fees for open access journal articles.  
 
The report does not describe efforts toward equitable public-private partnerships on open science, noting that measures will follow adoption of the national open science policy. 
 
No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

Under the MINERVA programme, the four participating universities developed their own scientific repositories. All types of academic theses (bachelor's, master's and doctoral) are also stored in open access by the National Library of Armenia.  
 
The Higher Education and Science Committee also maintains a portal listing research equipment of state research organisations. 

In addition, the report notes that Armenian research periodicals are mostly open access and that most maintain their own archives. It adds that some Armenian researchers, depending on their field, initially disseminate their work via archive.org repository.
 
Armenia does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, though existing international open science infrastructures are reported as accessible to relevant Armenian actors. 
 
No North-S​outh, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for open science platforms are reported.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place through the MINERVA programme. The four beneficiary universities continue to share their knowledge and competences within their own organisations and with other institutions after the programme's completion. 

 

No capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies are reported. The report attributes this to the relatively recent introduction of open science in Armenia, noting that no regular seminars for decision-makers have been established. 

 

A framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula according to the report, which notes that universities are still working to integrate the topic. The report does not describe the use of open educational resources for open science capacity building. No initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values are reported; the report indicates that such initiatives are expected after adoption of the national open science policy.

No clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems according to the report. Similarly, no initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported. In both cases, the report indicates that such measures are expected after the national open science policy is adopted. 

 

The report identifies predatory and fraudulent journals as an unintended negative consequence of open science practices. Armenian researchers have encountered difficulties identifying such journals. In response, the Higher Education and Science Committee provides consultations on whether journals appear on Beall's list of predatory publications and includes a provision in its calls for proposals that disqualifies publications in listed journals.

The report does not describe national or institutional initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase openness. It notes that such initiatives are expected after adoption of the national open science policy.

Regarding international scientific collaborations, most grant programmes administered by the Higher Education and Science Committee require the involvement of a foreign collaborator. Bilateral grant programmes also support joint research within the framework of international agreements. However, the information provided in the report has not clarified whether open science is directly integrated into these collaborations. 

 

No strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported. The report notes that such a strategy will be elaborated after a strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats (SWOT) analysis in this area. 

 

Armenia does not report involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science, citing a need for further research into potential collaborators and partners.

2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Sciencehas been promoted through several channels. The Australian Open Science Network has adopted the UNESCO definition of open science and works to promote the 2021 Recommendation. The 2021 Recommendation was also a key point of the Australian Roadmap for Open Research roundtable held on 13 June 2024 by the Academy of Social Sciences in Australia.

Awareness-raising activities on open science have been carried out by several entities. Open Access Australasia and the Council of Australian University Librarians (CAUL) actively promote open access and advocate for policies aligned with international standards. Australia's former Chief Scientist, Dr Cathy Foley, advocated for open access to research literature during her tenure and released a report making the case for a public access model to provide Australians with access to academic journal articles and enable open access publishing of Australian-led research. The Australian Open Science Network has held several webinars raising awareness on open science.

Australia has several national science, technology and innovation strategies that include open science elements, among them the National Science and Research Priorities and the National Science Statement. The report describes a single national-level open science policy or roadmap as under development, though specific policy instruments and mandates operate at the research funder level. The National Health and Medical Research Council (NHMRC) and the Medical Research Future Fund (MRFF)'s policy requires immediate open access to publications with a Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) licence and strongly encourages grant recipients to take reasonable steps to share research data and associated metadata. The Australian Research Council (ARC) Open Access Policy requires research outputs to be accessible within 12 months from the date of publication; the policy was reported to be under review for an update.

At institutional level, NHMRC's Open Access Policy encourages research institutions to develop their own institutional open science policies. Universities across Australia maintain varying open access policies.

The above-mentioned national research funders, as well as the  Australian Research Data Commons (ARDC) and the National Collaborative Research Infrastructure Strategy (NCRIS), support various aspects of open science. For example, NHMRC grant funding may be used to cover publication and open access costs.

 

Efforts to advance equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported through NCRIS, ARDC and Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO) collaborations, with increased efforts foreseen by the end of 2025.

 

The report does not describe a national monitoring framework for open science but envisages that existing policies and networks could lead to the development of one.

Australia maintains a range of national and institutional open science infrastructures. The NCRIS programme funds and coordinates open access research infrastructure facilities across multiple specialities, outlined at riconnected.org.au. Research Link Australia, a searchable database of researchers, projects and organisations, is one example of NCRIS-funded activity. The Australian Government also funds the Australian Synchrotron and hosts the Square Kilometre Array (SKA). Several institutions, including the National Library of Australia and universities, independently host open access journals as community-managed platforms.

 

Australia contributes to federated regional and international open science infrastructure through multiple channels. For example, several NCRIS-funded capabilities feed into international data systems, among them Bioplatforms Australia for genomic data, the Atlas of Living Australia contributing biodiversity data to the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF), the Terrestrial Ecosystem Research Network (TERN) and the Integrated Marine Observing System (IMOS). Australian institutions have also supported the Global Sustainability Coalition for Open Science Services (SCOSS).

 

Australia is engaged in North-South and regional collaborations to optimise infrastructure use, including the Landsat Next satellite programme, the Southern Ocean Observing System (SOOS) and CSIRO's Regional Partnerships in the Pacific and Southeast Asia.

Capacity building on open science for both researchers and policymakers draws on the 2021 National Research Infrastructure Roadmap, which identifies workforce development for open research infrastructure as a priority. This roadmap will continue to inform investments through 2025 before a new one is developed for 2026.
 
A framework of open science competencies in higher education research skills curricula is reported as under development.
 
The report affirms that open educational resources are used for open science capacity building. For example, the CAUL Open Education Resources Collective publishes open educational resources through Pressbooks.
 
On science communication, the Australian Government's 2024 National Science Statement calls for increased efforts to support open science and cross-disciplinary and cross-institutional collaborations.

On review of research assessment and evaluation, the Universities Accord Panel's Final Report recommends that open data be leveraged to support research evaluation processes (p. 220), though the Australian Government has not yet responded.

 

The NHMRC is a signatory of the 2012 San Francisco Declaration on Research Assessment (DORA) and the Agreement on Reforming Research Assessment, and is a member of the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA). It is developing an action plan aligned with CoARA's core commitments. NHMRC funding schemes that assess publication history require applicants not to include journal impact factors, and its Open Access Policy encourages research institutions to reward open science practices in recruitment, evaluation and promotion. NHMRC is also developing a Good Institutional Practice Guide that encourages recognising and rewarding open science.

 

The report states that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not been incorporated in career evaluation and progression systems, though this process is underway. Many institutions, including the University of New South Wales (UNSW), now adopt societal impact frameworks.

 

Regarding dedicated initiatives recognising and rewarding open science practices, some Australian journals have adopted Open Science Badges to acknowledge researchers who share data, materials or pre-register studies.

 

The report does not identify any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

Several national and institutional initiatives promote participatory and procedural changes to increase openness at different stages of the research cycle. The NHMRC Open Access Policy encourages the posting of preprints on preprint servers with open licensing, and NHMRC accepts preprints in grant applications. Through its Targeted Calls for Research scheme, NHMRC enables community and professional groups to submit research topics that may be underfunded or have a significant knowledge gap. Consumer and community representatives are routinely appointed to NHMRC Council and Principal Committees, and NHMRC is trialling the involvement of consumer and community reviewers in peer review for select grant opportunities.

 

For some MRFF grant opportunities, grantees are required to submit clinical trial protocols to an open access repository within six months of ethics approval and provide lay summaries to research participants concurrently with dissemination of results.

 

At institutional level, Australian universities have policies encouraging open peer review and sharing of negative results and associated data.

Australia promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through several channels. Australia's Nuclear Science and Technology Organisation (ANSTO) engages multilaterally through the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) and regionally through the Regional Cooperative Agreement for Research, Development and Training Related to Nuclear Science and Technology for Asia and the Pacific (RCA) and the Forum for Nuclear Cooperation in Asia (FNCA). ANSTO operates an Open Science Portal providing access to research data and hosts the Australian node of the IAEA International Nuclear Information System (INIS), which captures and indexes Australian nuclear science literature for free global access. CAUL has negotiated open access agreements with over 20 publishers since 2020.

 

The report describes a strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science as under development. The NHMRC participates in several international working groups, including the Asia-Pacific DORA Funders working group, cOAlition S Leaders and Experts committees, and CoARA working groups. NHMRC has also engaged with UNESCO Open Science working groups.

 

Australia participates in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science through DORA Funders and CoARA working groups. Open Access Australasia engages with Invest in Open Infrastructure and SCOSS, and CAUL also promotes SCOSS initiatives.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Sciencewas shared with the Federal Ministry responsible for Education, Science and Research, and a broad range of stakeholders at research and higher education institutions are reported to be aware of it. 
 
Several awareness-raising activities on open science have taken place between 2021 and 2024, with further initiatives planned for 2025. In October 2023, the Federal Ministry responsible for Education, Science and Research organised an international conference at the University of Vienna themed "5 Years of European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Developments" examining perspectives and challenges for data in European science and research. The Austrian Citizen Science Conference, the main annual event for the citizen science community since 2015. The University of Vienna organised an Open Science Festival in September 2025 for researchers, students and research support staff. Open Science Austria (OSA) is a platform dedicated to raising awareness of open science through the dissemination of information, as well as fostering coordination and networking among stakeholders.

RTI Strategy 2030 (Strategy for Research, Technology and Innovation of the Austrian Federal Government), adopted in 2020, references open science as a cross-cutting issue. The RTI Pacts for 2021–2023 and 2024–2026 and the Research Infrastructure Action Plan 2030 implement the RTI Strategy 2030's targets. The principal national policy is the Open Science Policy Austria (2022), collaboratively developed by the Federal Ministry of Education, Science and Research and two other federal ministries, addressing open access, Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) data, open educational resources (OER) and the EOSC; this policy constitutes Austria's dedicated national roadmap for open science. Additional relevant national policies include the Austrian Data Strategy (2024)and the and the Open Innovation Strategy for Austria (2016). 
 
At the institutional level, eleven universities, including the University of Vienna, have research data management policies encouraging FAIR principles, and several have adopted open access and OER policies. 
 
Among specific national funding mechanisms for open science, the Sparkling Science 2.0 programme, coordinated by Austria's Agency for Education and Internationalisation (OeAD), supports citizen science projects involving schools and societal partners. The report also states that open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding programmes through funding agencies’ policies. For example, the Austrian Science Fund (FWF) mandates open access for funded research outputs, requires data management plans and supports citizen science. The Austrian Research Promotion Agency (FFG) integrates open science requirements in some programmes, including for Horizon Europe projects. Open access publishing is also supported through 20 national-level agreements negotiated by the Austrian Academic Library Consortium and the Austrian Transition to Open Access projects (2017–2020 and 2021–2025). 
 
The report states that efforts to build equitable public-private partnerships on open science are currently limited, citing the absence of a dedicated framework and challenges in aligning private-sector objectives with open science principles. 
 
Regarding national monitoring of open science, a working group at the EOSC Support Office Austria monitors national EOSC processes and regularly updates a country status report. In addition, the Austrian Datahub for Open Access Negotiations and Monitoring monitors the nationwide open access status of publications.

Austria is one of the few countries worldwide which maintains a public research infrastructure database listing approximately 2,500 infrastructures open for shared use as of February 2025.  

 

Federated information technology infrastructures include the Austrian Social Science Data Archive (AUSSDA), which provides data archiving and reuse services, and the Cloud Infrastructure Platform (CLIP), which offers interdisciplinary high-performance computing jointly operated by the Austrian Academy of Sciences and partner institutes in Vienna. The Austrian NeuroCloud, a FAIR-enabling open repository for neurocognitive data across locations, and the university-led project Open Education Austria Advanced, which develops a national OER infrastructure, are further examples. Among platforms for co-creation of knowledge between scientists and society, the citizen science platform Österreich forscht listed over 90 active projects at the end of 2024.  

 

Austria also hosts federated international open science infrastructure, such as Monitoring Electoral Democracy (MEDem), a European research infrastructure based at the University of Vienna, which harmonises democracy research data across Europe in line with FAIR principles, with support from over 60 institutions in more than 40 countries.

Systematic capacity building on open science takes place across Austrian higher education and research institutions through both formal and informal channels. The University of Innsbruck offers an Open Science Summer School and a Data Librarian university course. At the University of Vienna, capacity building centres on a university-wide research data management (RDM) network and Data Stewardship programme. The University of Natural Resources and Life Sciences Vienna (Universität für Bodenkultur Wien, BOKU), which coordinates the Citizen Science Network Austria and its associated “Österreich forscht" platform, has integrated citizen science training into staff development courses since 2019, recently opening these to members of other universities. The OeAD provides training including the OpenAIRE (Open Access Infrastructure for Research in Europe) Open Science Bootcamp and citizen science webinars. 

 

The report indicates that no standardised framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula across Austria. Individual institutions are integrating open science into their strategic plans, and the Digital Skills Initiative for Austria promotes digital literacy more broadly. An Austrian OER certification for higher education institutions and their employees is under development. 

 

The report affirms that OER are used as an instrument for open science capacity building. 

 

Several science communication initiatives align with open science values. The Federal Ministry of Education, Science and Research launched "DNAustria" in 2024, a platform enabling the public to find science and democracy education programmes across all federal states. The Austrian Academy of Sciences initiated "FAEKT!", a youth science communication programme connecting scientists with young people through videos and social media. The non-profit association “Diskurs. Das Wissenschaftsnetz", founded in 2019 by scientists in Austria, connects scientific knowledge with mass media through press conferences presenting new research.

A 2024 study commissioned by Open Science Australia (OSA) found that open science practices are only marginally considered in the appointment of new professors at Austrian higher education institutions. Specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression have not yet been widely incorporated at the institutional level. In response, OSA founded the Research Assessment (II) working group in December 2024 to develop evaluation criteria and issue national recommendations. The report of this working group on career paths in science and research assessment calls on all actors in Austrian higher education to engage in and value open science activities. At the institutional level, the University of Vienna's "Concepts on Evaluating Research Activities and Publications” include recommendations for open access publishing and the use of persistent identifiers such as Digital Object Identifier (DOI) and Open Researcher and Contributor Identifier (ORCID). Several Austrian higher education and research institutions have joined the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA). 

 

Among initiatives to recognise and reward open science, the Sparkling Science 2.0 funding programme was highlighted. It promotes collaboration between research institutions, schools and societal actors and requires free access to results and datasets. Coordinated by the OeAD, calls are planned every two years. 

 

The report does not document specific unintended negative consequences of open science in Austria, though it notes potential challenges. Predatory publishing is identified as a concern; the Austrian Transition to Open Access 2 (AT2OA²) project (2021–2024) addressed it through a subproject that led to the creation of "wisskomm", a community of practice on quality in open access publishing.

Austria has taken steps at both national and institutional levels to promote innovative approaches at different stages of the research cycle. Austrian researchers participate in preprint networks such as arXiv and bioRxiv. In December 2024, the FWF and nine other European research funding organisations signed a Statement of Intent to support the Open Research Europe publication platform. The FWF also encourages open peer-review practices, including transparent reviewer identities. Austrian initiatives promote the sharing of negative scientific results through platforms such as the Open Science Framework and Open Research Europe.  

 

To promote open innovation practices, Austria adopted the Open Innovation Strategy (2016), including measures to embed open data and open access in research to foster cross-sector collaboration, while in parallel implementing the European Council Recommendation on the guiding principles for knowledge valorisation, including the 2024 Codes of Practice on citizen engagement and industry–academia co-creation. This approach is reflected in initiatives such as the Open Innovation in Science Center of the Ludwig Boltzmann Gesellschaft, which promotes collaborative research with citizens and stakeholders, particularly in the health sector, to enhance social impact. 

 

Participatory methods are supported through citizen science. The Citizen Science Network Austria facilitates public participation, and the Sparkling Science 2.0 programme integrates schools and citizens into research, including work on engaging hard-to-reach groups.

Austria promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through its active participation in the EOSC Austria that supports Austrian researchers in aligning with European open science standards and developing shared infrastructures. In addition, the country is a member of several European and international research infrastructures.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been shared with the Ministry of Culture, the Ministry of Economics, the Latvian Council of Science and the Latvian National Library.

 Awareness-raising activities on open science are organised through the Research in Latvia platform, which features open science as a theme in science communication events such as the 5th World Congress of Latvian Scientists. At the institutional level, the University of Latvia, Riga Technical University and Riga Stradiņš University have dedicated staff working on open science who organise events for the academic community.

Latvia's national science, technology and innovation policy, the Guidelines for Science, Technology Development and Innovation 2021–2027, incorporates open science elements, including open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure and open engagement of societal actors.

 The Latvian Open Science Strategy 2021–2027 is the national-level dedicated policy on open science. It includes an Action Plan that provides the national implementation roadmap.

 Specific policy instruments to promote open science carry an allocated budget of 21.92 million euros for the 2021–2027 period.

 Regarding institutional-level promotion of open science, the Open Science Strategy's Action Plan includes activities directed at research performing organisations.

 Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms: publicly funded research carries mandatory requirements for green open access for all publications and for publishing Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) research data in line with data management plans submitted by researchers.

 The report does not describe efforts toward equitable public-private partnerships on open science.

 As part of national monitoring for open science, the Ministry of Education and Science monitors open access publications and published research datasets.

Latvia reports national open science infrastructures in the category of federated information technology infrastructure. These include a state information system of scientific activities, serving as a register of institutions, researchers, projects, results, equipment, among other elements, as well as a national Dataverse network currently being established.

 Latvia does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, though existing regional and international open science infrastructures are reported as accessible to relevant open science actors in the country.

 Latvia does not report involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use.

Systematic capacity building on open science has included trainings on open science and research data stewardship. A network of 20 data stewards is also being introduced to work in research performing organisations.

 No capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies are reported.

 Open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula; the report notes this falls under the responsibility of higher education institutions.

 The report indicates that open educational resources are not used as an instrument for open science capacity building.

 Science communication initiatives aligned with open science include events organised through the Research in Latvia platform.

Latvia has reviewed research assessment and evaluation processes through the cyclical International Evaluation of Scientific Institution Activity, which assesses all research performing organisations and included open access in its 2020 round. The next round is planned to focus more on open data and societal engagement.

 Specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems are reported as foreseen for the next evaluation round.

 Initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices include prize competitions organised by universities, such as one held at the University of Latvia.

 The report does not identify any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

The National Open Science Strategy promotes innovative approaches at the national level to increase openness across the research cycle. Its scope encompasses the promotion of preprints, open peer review practices and the valuing of negative scientific results and associated data. The Strategy also addresses participatory methods, including citizen science and crowdsourcing, and open innovation practices.

Latvia's international scientific collaboration on open science occurs primarily through the European Union's research and innovation framework programme, Horizon Europe, where open science principles are fully integrated.

 Latvia does not report a dedicated strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, citing the country's small size as a reason for not having such a document.

 Regarding discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, Latvia reports direct involvement as an EU member state in establishing EU-level mechanisms, including through the European Open Science Cloud.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted to the Research Council of Lithuania. The report does not describe awareness-raising activities on open science organised or foreseen by national authorities by the end of 2025.

According to the report, Lithuania's national science, technology and innovation policy addresses open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure and open engagement of societal actors. Open science elements are also partly addressed through national-level policies and legal frameworks, such as Article 51 of the Law on Research and Higher Education, amended in 2024, which requires publicly funded research and development results to be made available through open access, subject to exceptions for protection of personal data, intellectual property and national security. This framework also includes the Research Council of Lithuania's Description of the Procedure for Open Access to Research and Development Results, adopted in 2024.

Both specific policy instruments and a national implementation plan for open science are under development. The Research Council plans to develop an action plan for open access to research and development results, including harmonisation of related procedures and a monitoring procedure, by the end of 2026. However, the report notes that open access requirements are being introduced cautiously, as geopolitical changes require greater concern for security and autonomy.

At institutional level, the above-mentioned Research Council’s Description of the Procedure establishes principles for open access to publications and data, recommends on repositories, guides research and higher education institutions on incentives and citizen involvement and sets monitoring areas. Its provisions will be applied gradually.

Specific national funding mechanisms for open science include government financial support for participation in international research infrastructure, covering membership fees and activities. In particular, two programmes are under development: Encourage Participation in International Research Infrastructures and Integration into the European Open Science Cloud. Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding: open access expenses are eligible costs in research and development projects, and requirements for the dissemination of publications, events and data management plans are incorporated into funding schemes.

The report states that no concrete action has been agreed on equitable public-private partnerships for open science, though the issue is under discussion.

A national monitoring framework for open science is under development. Monitoring areas are set in the Research Council’s Description of the Procedure, and a detailed monitoring procedure is planned by 2026.

Lithuanian universities and academic libraries host six open data repositories. Two of these are members of European Research Infrastructure Consortia: the Consortium of European Social Science Data Archives (CESSDA-ERIC) and the Common Language Resources and Technology Infrastructure (CLARIN-ERIC). Lithuania also participates in eight other European research infrastructures, including the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN) and the Biobanking and BioMolecular Resources Research Infrastructure (BBMRI-ERIC). The reported open science infrastructure spans two categories: federated information technology infrastructure and community-managed infrastructures, protocols and standards.

 Lithuania hosts the Lithuanian Grid Infrastructure for High-Performance Computing (LitGrid-HPC), which contributes to international HPC infrastructures.

 Regarding North-South and South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use, the report cites Lithuania's participation in European research infrastructures.

Systematic capacity building on open science at research and higher education institutions includes studies in information and communications technology and the training of data stewards. The planned Integration into the European Open Science Cloud programme is also expected to develop competences in research data.

 The report states that no capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have been provided, owing to the small size of the administration. Policymaker competences are instead developed through participation in open science events.

 Open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education curricula through dedicated courses on open science for doctoral students.

 The report does not describe the use of open educational resources for open science capacity building.

 No initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values are reported. The report notes that discussions are ongoing but concrete solutions are not yet available owing to resource constraints.

Regarding research assessment and evaluation, institutions in the humanities earn additional points for scientifically structured datasets in annual evaluations. The points earned for research outputs, including structured datasets, determine incentive funding for research, development and arts at universities and research institutes.

 Open science principles have been incorporated in career evaluation. The Research Council of Lithuania includes them in its approved list of desirable competences for researchers.

 Initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices include the Open Science Awards established by Vilnius University in September 2024.

 The report does not indicate any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

The report indicates that there are no initiatives at the national or institutional level promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes to increase openness across the research cycle.

Lithuania plans to implement a programme for integration into the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC). This programme is intended to upgrade scientific data repositories, build capacity, strengthen coordination of data management at the national level and promote integration into the EOSC.

 The report states that Lithuania does not have a specific strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. While the country promotes the internationalisation of research broadly, no specific open science activities have been foreseen owing to limited resources.

 Lithuania does not report involvement in discussions on regional or international funding mechanisms for open science, and no such activities are currently planned.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research and the National Centre for Scientific and Technological Research.

 Awareness-raising activities on open science have taken place. The first edition of the Science Festival (Fête des Sciences), held in 2016, was organised under the theme "Open Science, leave no one behind".

The report states that the national science, technology and innovation policy addresses open access to scientific knowledge as an open science element, though no details are provided. No additional national-level policy or legal framework addressing open science is reported, with gaps attributed to insufficient funds and a lack of human resources. No specific policy instruments or national implementation plan or roadmap for open science are reported, and institutional-level open science policies are likewise absent, although training on open science is mentioned as a planned step.  

 Specific national funding mechanisms for open science are reported as under development. The report cites Competitive Research Funds and the creation of a Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics. Open science practices have not been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms, according to the report, owing to difficulties in allocating funds for open science and a lack of predictability.

 No efforts toward equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported. The report cites insufficient funding and a need to strengthen the National Centre for Scientific and Technological Research.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is reported as under development, though no details are provided.

The report states that there are no national or institutional open science infrastructures. Mali does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, and existing open science infrastructures at regional and international levels are reported as not accessible to relevant open science actors in the country. No North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use for open science platforms are reported.

No systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education or research-performing institutions, according to the report, which attributes this to a lack of funding and the absence of open science infrastructure. Capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values and principles into science, technology and innovation policies have not taken place; the report signals a need to strengthen policymaker capacities in this area.

 No framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report, which notes an intention to introduce open science modules into education programmes. Open educational resources are not reported as being used for open science capacity building. No science communication initiatives in line with open science values are reported, with the report citing financial difficulties as the reason.

No initiatives to review existing research assessment and evaluation processes in line with open science principles are reported. The report cites the absence of a funding mechanism and the lack of a research environment conducive to talent expression. No specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems, according to the report; it identifies a need for a dialogue space between researchers to establish a shared vision of research priorities.

 No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported. The report points to material and human difficulties in organising training sessions to promote an open science culture and competitions to reward open science practices.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates that initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle exist at both national and institutional levels. The areas addressed include the promotion of preprints, the valuing and sharing of negative scientific results and associated data, the development of participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities and incorporating socially relevant issues into research agendas, and the promotion of open innovation practices. No further details are provided in the report.

Mali reports facilitating international scientific collaboration on open science through partnerships with higher education and research institutions of the Confederation of Sahel States, which comprises Mali, Niger and Burkina Faso. A Conference of Heads of Higher Education and Research Institutions of the Confederation was planned for the end of April 2025 in Bamako.

 No strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported. The report cites the absence of a long-term research strategy and a need to strengthen the National Centre for Scientific and Technological Research for the dissemination and collection of scientific and technological information.

 Mali does not report involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not been explicitly shared with state institutions and scientific organisations, although relevant government agencies, ministries and their research and academic institutions have access to it. 

 

Although the Recommendation has not been formally disseminated to state institutions, awareness activities have nevertheless been conducted. These activities date back to January 2020, when the Institute of Education hosted an Open Science Workshop with international experts for 60 participants from public and private universities. In 2022, the Open Science Azerbaijan project held workshops at Baku State University, Azerbaijan University of Languages and Azerbaijan University of Technology to inform young researchers about open access, open data and scientific networks. The Institute of Physics, under the Ministry of Science and Education, held a seminar on open science in May 2023. In October 2024, the Azerbaijan Society of Young Scientists, Postgraduates and Masters organised an Open Science Summer School for Young Researchers in Lankaran, and in December 2024 Azerbaijan Technical University hosted an event on open access and science diplomacy under the Development of Research Potential through the Institutional Repositories Network in Azerbaijan (DIRNA) project, funded through the Erasmus+ Key Action 2 programme. That same month, Baku State University opened the International Open Science and Education Laboratory.

Azerbaijan's science, technology and innovation policy addresses open science through the Law "On Science" (2016), which defines state policy principles in scientific activity and stipulates that state-funded research is open to the public. Although the term “open science” is not explicitly used, the law incorporates elements of open science, is aligned with the 2021 Recommendation and serves as the main policy instrument for its promotion.   
 
At institutional level, universities and research performing institutions support open science through various workshops, seminars and capacity building projects, including Open Science Azerbaijan. However, the report does not describe any formal policy documents. 
 
The report does not identify specific national funding mechanisms for open science. However, it indicates that open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding, which the Law "On Science" organises into base financing for fundamental research, programme-targeted financing for strategic scientific work and grant financing to stimulate research competitiveness; the report provides no further detail. 
 
The report indicates that there is no national monitoring framework for open science.

National open science infrastructures are reported to be under development. Technoparks with facilities for conducting scientific research and experimental design have been created and are open to scientists. There is a consolidated official list of state-owned and state-funded research infrastructure that informs researchers of available equipment.  

 

The report states that Azerbaijan does not host or fund federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Azerbaijan does not report involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for open science platforms.

The report states that no systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education or research-performing institutions. 
 
Capacity building specifically for those who shape science policy is reported to be under consideration. 
 
The report states that open science competencies have not yet been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula. Plans exist to develop educational programmes at master's level that incorporate such competencies. 
 
Open educational resources are used partly as an instrument for open science capacity building, though the report provides no further detail. 
 
The report indicates that several initiatives support science communication in line with open science values. The Ministry of Science and Education holds an annual Republican Scientific Conference for Doctoral Students and Young Researchers to discuss early-career research results and stimulate scientific creativity. The state has also established the Presidential Award for Youth (Gənclər üçün Prezident Mükafatı) and the Youth Award (Gənclər mükafatı) to recognise young talents. However, the information provided in the report has not clarified how open science is integrated into these initiatives.

With regard to reviewing existing research assessment and evaluation processes in order to align them with open science principles and values, the report cited an accreditation procedure that determines the quality and effectiveness of scientific programmes and research results, conducted under rules approved by the Cabinet of Ministers in Decision No. 554 of 14 December 2017; however, no further details are provided. 

 

The report does not identify any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

No initiatives are reported at the national or institutional level to promote innovative or participatory methodological changes at different stages of the research cycle. The report notes that a Presidential Decree of 28 July 2022 entrusted the Ministry of Science and Education with implementing state policy in science and coordinating the activities of scientific institutions, and that an action plan is to be prepared.

International scientific collaborations on open science are reported to occur primarily at institutional level, with various scientific and higher education institutions implementing projects in this area. 

 

The report states that no strategy or plan exists for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. A Presidential Decree of 28 July 2022 centralised science policy authority in the Ministry of Science and Education, and an action plan is to be developed. 

 

Azerbaijan does not report involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science, noting that relevant research is planned for the future.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted across Belgium's governance levels. At the federal level, the Belgian Science Policy Office (Belspo) informed its Direction for Federal, Interfederal and International Coordination and the ten Federal Scientific Institutions (FSIs), and references the Recommendation on its website. In Flanders, the Department of Work, Economy, Science, Innovation and Social Economy (WEWIS) discussed it with stakeholders in the Flemish Open Science Board (FOSB) at the time of publication and continues to promote its content through the regional open science policy. The Federation Wallonia-Brussels (FWB) treats the Recommendation as a reference text guiding its open science efforts, cited in its Open Science Road Map. In the Brussels-Capital Region, the innovation agency Innoviris uses it as a source document for its open science encouragement policy, though Innoviris reports the Recommendation was not actively promoted to ministries.

 Awareness-raising activities span all levels. Under the Belgian Presidency of the EU Council in April 2024, Belspo, WEWIS and the FWB co-organised a national European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) tripartite event  in Brussels to exchange best practices on open science and open data. In Flanders, universities and most research institutions hold open science events regularly. The Flemish Research Data Network (FRDN) organised a stakeholder network day in 2023 and 2025. A Belgian event on open access, predatory practices and copyright was organised by Open Access Belgium, an inter-stakeholder collaboration, in December 2024. The FWB has operated a Data Ambassadors network at its five universities since 2021 for collaborative learning on open and Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) research data management.

Belgium's federated structure means science policy is shared between federal and regional authorities, with no single national policy. At the federal level, the Belspo’s Strategic Plan 2022-2024 commits to open data, FAIR data and the EOSC, supported by Belspo open access  and open research data mandates and a Secondary Publishing Right law (Art. 29). In Flanders, the Flemish Open Science policy plan (2019-2024, extended to 2025) is the regional open science implementation roadmap, developed with all 36 research institutions, the Research Foundation – Flanders (FWO) and Flanders Innovation and Entrepreneurship (VLAIO). Within the French-speaking community in Wallonia and Brussels, the FWB adopted an open access decree in 2018 requiring the deposit of publicly-funded publications in institutional repositories immediately after acceptance by a publisher, and it maintains the Open Science Road Map. The Brussels-Capital Region embeds open science in its Regional Innovation Plan 2021-2027.

 Examples of specific policy instruments at the federal level include Belspo's Open Science Helpdesk and the Federal Open Science Strategy Committee (FOSSC). The latter was established in 2024 to advise the board of directors of Belspo on federal open science policy.

 

As examples of institutional open science policies, many Flemish research institutions, including Ghent University and KU Leuven, have developed internal policies, stimulated by the Flemish Open Science policy plan. These policies cover aspects such as open access, institutional repositories and research data management. In addition, most universities within the Federation Wallonia-Brussels have developed their own policies.

 As an example of specific funding mechanism for open science, in Flanders a €5 million budget is allocated across 36 research institutions for infrastructure and data steward personnel, linked to the Flemish Open Science policy plan.

 

Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms. At the federal level, BELSPO research contracts require open access deposit. In Flanders, funding instruments from FWO and VLAIO include open science requirements, with FWO also covering expenses for gold open access publications, and similar provisions reflected in institution-specific funding agreements concluded by WEWIS with Flemish research institutions. Within the French-speaking community of Wallonia and Brussels, the funding agency, the Board of Trustees of the F.R.S.-FNRS, has incorporated open science practices into project evaluation procedures. In the Brussels-Capital Region, Innoviris currently applies open science requirements in a limited number of research funding programmes, with a generalised policy planned for 2025.    

 The report states that no efforts have been made toward equitable public-private partnerships, as federal open science policies currently concern only public institutions.

 The report highlights that open science monitoring frameworks are in place in Flanders and within the French-speaking community of Wallonia and Brussels. In Flanders, monitoring relies on yearly institutional reports tracking indicators such as Open Researcher and Contributor ID (ORCID) identifiers, data management plans, open access and open data, supported by the Flemish Research Information Space (FRIS), which enables automated tracking. The FWB publishes an annual report on the effects of its 2018 open access decree and the University of Liège has developed the PeriscOApe tool to monitor open access levels as part of the evaluation of the FWB decree’s impact.

At the federal level, Orfeo is a centralised repository for open access publications, targeted primarily at the FSIs, administrated by Belspo and hosted by the Belgian National Research and Education Network (Belnet). DMP Online, also hosted by Belnet, helps researchers generate data management plans. SODHA is a federal archive that preserves research data for social sciences and digital humanities and represents Belgium in the Consortium of European Social Science Data Archives (CESSDA). Belspo has developed a citizen science platform for the projects of the FSIs.

 

In Flanders, FRIS provides a regional information portal on researchers and their research, universities maintain their own repositories, and the Flemish Supercomputer Centre (Vlaams Supercomputer Centrum, VSC) provides high-performance computing. The FWB launched PeriScops in 2025, a portal harvesting metadata from all five university repositories of scientific publications. The RéFéR directory gives access to publications produced or funded by the FWB ministry. Reliade, a French-speaking Belgian network of diamond open access journals launched in 2024.

 

Regarding hosting or funding of federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, Belgium reported, as an example, its above-mentioned regional FRIS portal, which covers all publicly funded research in Flanders.

 

Regarding North-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use, the Meise Botanic Garden (Plantentuin Meise) actively supports the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF).

Systematic capacity building on open science takes place at multiple levels. The Federal Open Science Cloud (FedOSC) project, launched in April 2024, aims to develop digital infrastructure, create a policy framework and provide guidance and training for the FSIs. In Flanders, the Flemish Open Science policy plan funds data steward positions at research institutions, and the FRDN facilitates knowledge exchange among research data management support staff. The FWB has operated a Data Ambassadors network at its all five universities since 2022, and training courses are available.
 
No capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies are reported.
 
Regarding open science competencies in higher education curricula, Flemish doctoral schools, for example, offer open science training as part of the skill set for young researchers, and data stewards at institutions provide needs-based guidance.
 
Open educational resources are partly used for open science capacity building. In Flanders, within the FRDN, institutions share best practices and openly available teaching materials. A collaborative educational game is being co-developed among FRDN members, building on KU Leuven's Research Survival Game. The University of Liège has developed OpenScientoMètre, a game to test and deepen open science knowledge.
 
In Flanders, a Science Communication policy plan (2022-2030) supports various actors including Scivil, the Flemish knowledge centre on citizen science, and FWO requires researchers to publish a popularising text in Dutch on their findings as a funding condition.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes are under way at several levels. At the federal level, Belspo's Open Access mandate requires publications financed by Belspo to be deposited in institutional repositories or in the federal repository Orfeo in order to be considered in researchers' evaluations, career advancement and job applications. In Flanders, the project group "Enriching Research Assessment with Open Science Practices", operating under the governance of the Open Science policy plan, recently concluded its work and produced recommendations approved by all stakeholders. Steps are now being taken to further include open science, open data and FAIR criteria in FWO's assessment of research projects, although researchers have for some time been allowed to report to FWO certain open science efforts. Some Flemish universities encourage researchers to cite open science contributions when seeking appointment or promotion. In addition, the F.R.S.-FNRS Board of Trustees has approved the valorisation of open science practices in its project evaluation procedures.

 

Several unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported. The evaluation of the Flemish Open Science policy plan 2019-2024 found that reporting on key performance indicators creates a large administrative burden, particularly for smaller institutions. Researchers at times experience conflicting expectations between open science and valorisation. In the FWB, monitoring reports on the 2018 open access decree indicate an increase in article processing charges.

Innovative participatory approaches are promoted at both federal, regional and institutional levels, particularly in citizen science. Belspo has developed a citizen science platform for the FSIs, and the Royal Museum for Central Africa (AfricaMuseum) organises citizen science webinars, workshops and an annual seminar bringing together researchers and citizen scientists. In Flanders, WEWIS funds Scivil, the Flemish knowledge centre on citizen science, which supports researchers in designing such projects, and the AMAI! initiative is dedicated to citizen science projects linked to artificial intelligence.

 

Preprints and open peer review have been discussed at institutional open science events in Flanders, though practices vary between disciplines and are not yet prominent in institutional policies. Sharing of negative scientific results has been raised during the evaluation of the Flemish Open Science policy plan and is being considered for the next policy period. In open innovation, VLAIO supports living labs and the Blikopener initiative enables universities of applied sciences and arts to engage with companies.

Belgium promotes international collaboration on open science through several channels. Belspo is a board member of the Council for National Open Science Coordination (CoNOSC), a network of national open science coordinators in the UN-European region, and serves as National Point of Reference for Open Access in an EU Commission expert group. WEWIS provides the Belgian delegate to the EOSC Steering Board, and several Belgian research institutions are members of the EOSC Association. Belnet facilitates recurring meetings with these stakeholders.

 

The report states that no dedicated strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science exists. However, in addition to above-mentioned initiatives, Belgian institutions participate in and provide co-funding opportunities for several European projects (e.g., DiSSCo: an European research infrastructure for natural science collections).

Federal departments and agencies were informed of the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science during its development and upon adoption. Canada established a working group of federal open science actors to inform negotiations, while the Canadian Commission for UNESCO (CCUNESCO) contributed civil society perspectives to the Recommendation. The Recommendation was discussed in bodies including the Deputy Ministers' Roadmap for Open Science Steering Committee. In Quebec, the Fonds de recherche du Québec (FRQ) and the provincial open data portal Données Québec served as promotion channels.

 Awareness-raising activities span federal and provincial levels. At the level of federal government departments and agencies, Natural Resources Canada established an Open Science Committee in 2021 to organise awareness sessions for scientists and senior management, while the Public Health Agency of Canada launched an Open Science Community of Practice with a website, quarterly newsletter and membership in the Open Researcher and Contributor ID (ORCID) Canada Consortium. Environment and Climate Change Canada maintains internet and intranet sites on open science and operates an internal working group. In addition, CCUNESCO co-hosted a nine-part global webinar series in 2020, Open Science and the Decolonization of Knowledge, featuring nearly 20 Indigenous Elders and scholars. In 2022, it partnered with UNESCO to publish a plain language introduction to the Recommendation, sharing open-source files with other National Commissions for republication. In Quebec, the Commission on Ethics in Science and Technology published analyses of the benefits and ethical risks of open science, including in the context of COVID-19 and scientific data sharing, and presented this work to the provincial government and at international events.

At the federal level, Canada's 2014 Science, Technology, and Innovation Strategy incorporates open science elements, with commitments to the openness of federal science and publicly funded research articulated in successive National Action Plans on Open Government. The 2020 Roadmap for Open Science, from the Office of the Chief Science Advisor, serves as an overarching roadmap for implementing open science. Under this Roadmap, twelve federal science-based departments and agencies developed open science action plans to operationalise its objectives. In addition, the Model Policy on Scientific Integrity (2018) promotes transparency in all elements of the research process and requires federal departments and agencies to establish their scientific integrity policies that include provisions for the timely release of scientific information. At the provincial government level, for example, the 2022–2027 Quebec Research and Innovation Investment Strategy addresses the issue of access to research findings in the form of scientific publications or raw research data and fosters participatory and citizen science.

 

At the level of research performing institutions and universities, the Université de Montréal, for example, hosts the first UNESCO Chair on Open Science, and the Tanenbaum Open Science Institute (TOSI) at the Montreal Neurological Institute-Hospital (McGill University) drives the adoption of open science policies and practices across Canadian neuroscience research institutes.

 

Specific federal funding mechanisms include departmental Open Access Funds at Health Canada and the Public Health Agency of Canada, covering article processing charges. Examples at the provincial level include the Partnership for Open Access (POA), created by the digital publishing platform Érudit and the Canadian Research Knowledge Network (CRKN), which supports over 250 non-commercial diamond open access journals. At the same time, open science is integrated into existing research funding. Federal examples include the 2015 Tri-Agency Open Access Policy on Publications(revised in 2025 to mandate immediate open access), as well as the Tri-Agency Research Data Management Policy (which sets requirements for data management plans and data deposit), both issued by the three federal research granting agencies. In Quebec, the FRQ's Open Access Dissemination Policy (2019, revised 2022) requires immediate open access under an open licence for its funded works.

 

Regarding initiatives promoting equitable public-private partnerships on open science, the report cites the research promotion body Axelys in Quebec, which aims to maximise dissemination and societal use of knowledge from public research.

 

Regarding monitoring frameworks for open science in Canada, the federal 2020 Roadmap requires federal science-based departments to include performance measurement in their open science action plans. Canada's National Action Plan on Open Government tracks several open science indicators measuring the implementation of its commitments across federal departments and agencies. In addition, the Federal Science Libraries Network (FSLN) has been preparing annual Open Science Dashboards for member departments.

Various open science infrastructures are operated across federal, provincial and institutional levels, as illustrated by the following examples. The Federal Open Science Repository of Canada (FOSRC), launched in January 2024, provides public access to scientific publications from eight participating federal departments and agencies; the Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable (FAIR) and Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility and Ethics (CARE) principles were considered in its development, though no full compliance assessment has been conducted. Other federal repositories for publications include Natural Resources Canada's Open Science and Technology Repository and the National Research Council Canada Publications Archive. Canada's Open Government Portal hosts thousands of datasets (including research data), and the Open Science and Data Platform, co-led by Natural Resources Canada and Environment and Climate Change Canada, provides access to scientific publications, geospatial data and information on development activities. The Canadian Integrated Ocean Observing System (CIOOS) provides open access to ocean-related data aggregated from multiple sources across Canada, including federal government, academic and non-governmental organisations.

 

In Quebec, the open data portal Données Québec features datasets in twelve thematic areas. The provincial government funds SecureData4Health, a secure cloud infrastructure for genomic and health data sharing and analysis. The digital publishing infrastructure Érudit, based on free software and the FAIR principles, disseminates scholarly journals in the arts, humanities and social sciences under the diamond open access model. TOSI supports multiple neurological research platforms, including the Canadian Open Neuroscience Platform.

 

With regard to the hosting or funding federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, the report cites Érudit. Operating primarily in English and French, the platform receives over 35 million visits annually, approximately 70% from outside Canada.

 

Regarding the involvement in North–South and other forms of international collaboration to optimise shared infrastructure use, Canada contributed to the 2024 G20-facilitated process for the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF), supporting the expansion of open biodiversity databases through programmes such as Biodiversity Information for Development (BID).

Capacity building on open science takes place across federal institutions and Quebec bodies. The FSLN is developing a tool to guide researchers in selecting open access journals for publication that are covered by its transformative agreements. The National Research Council Canada's library built a guide titled "Open Science 101 For Researchers". The Public Health Agency of Canada provides training for researchers on ORCID, open access journals and contribution to the FOSRC repository. In Quebec, the FRQ has offered systematic training on its Open Access Dissemination Policy to grant holders since 2023. TOSI runs an annual Neuro Open Science in Action Symposium, and Érudit organises knowledge mobilisation events for journal editorial teams.

 

Capacity building for policymakers occurs through a federal interdepartmental working group chaired by Environment and Climate Change Canada, where participants share practices and challenges in implementing open science bimonthly. In Quebec, the Chief Scientist of Québec advises the provincial government. Érudit actively works to inform decision makers at various levels.

 

Open educational resources are used for open science capacity building. The University of Alberta Library's open science materials are licensed under Creative Commons, as are the resources at the Montreal Neurological Institute-Hospital, McGill University.

 

Regarding initiatives supporting science communication in line with open science values, the FOSRC repository is implementing plain language summaries to reach broader audiences. Environment and Climate Change Canada runs programmes such as the Ask a Scientist video series and the Scientist-at-large initiative, which connects scientists with the public through speaking engagements.
