The Public Health Agency of Canada and Health Canada jointly publish The Science of Health, a blog series presenting scientific activities in plain language. In Quebec, the FRQ organises events on scientific dissemination, and the provincial government hosts free virtual knowledge transfer meetings such as the Forest knowledge rendezvous.

Federal science-based departments and agencies are moving toward aligning research assessment with open science principles, particularly by integrating them in research funding schemes. Through the Quebec provincial government, the FRQ joined the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (COARA) and plans to review its assessment processes in the near future.

 

Career evaluation and progression for federal researchers is guided by the Career Progression Management Framework for Federal Researchers (2006), which lists collaborative research and public outreach among expected productivity outputs. Within this context, some departments provide further guidance; for example, at Natural Resources Canada, scientists can submit evidence of open science activities when presenting dossiers for career advancement. The Public Health Agency of Canada, with input from other federal departments, is reviewing the Framework's promotion criteria to explicitly incorporate open science, with revisions not expected until at least 2026–27.

 

TOSI offers Open Science awards and prizes that focus on qualitative impact rather than the number of publications.

 

Several unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported. In particularly, the federal science community expresses concern that, as more datasets become openly available, those carrying restrictions under Ownership, Control, Access and Possession (OCAP) principles asserted by Indigenous communities may be less likely to be used, potentially marginalising Indigenous perspectives. Quebec identifies a funding gap for diamond open access journals and notes that the absence of recognition for open access in researcher evaluation standards deters adoption. The report also observes that free dissemination does not guarantee that knowledge benefits the community that generated it.

Several repositories accept preprints, including the FOSRC and Natural Resources Canada's Open Science and Technology Repository. Participatory science is promoted through Canada's Citizen Science Portal, a national compilation of projects, and through departmental initiatives. The Public Health Agency of Canada uses citizen science for disease surveillance via FluWatchers and the Tiger Mosquito Citizen Science Study. Health Canada committed in the Fifth National Action Plan on Open Government 2022–2024 to develop infrastructure for citizen science in a health context. In 2023, it concluded a study on barriers and motivators to public participation in government-led research, especially among underrepresented groups, and is now working on a prototype participatory research exchange platform. In Quebec, the FRQ funds participatory research through programmes including ENGAGE, and specific calls for proposals address marginalised communities' needs, such as “Cultural safety, experience and needs of First Nations and Inuit users of the health and social services network”. In addition, in Quebec, the research promotion body Axelys is expected to enable a greater diversity of stakeholders to benefit from the societal impacts of scientific knowledge.

Canada promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through several channels. The Canadian Space Agency has partnered with the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) since 2017 on the International Space Apps Challenge, the world's largest  annual global hackathon using open data from space. Canada signed the Artemis Accords in October 2020, under which signatories pledge to share scientific data openly and publicly release research results. Fisheries and Oceans Canada supports the United Nations Decade of Ocean Science for Sustainable Development and participates in bodies including the International Council for the Exploration of the Sea (ICES), which developed a Transparent Assessment Framework as an open data resource, and the North Pacific Marine Science Organization. The FRQ is a member of cOAlition S, an international consortium of granting agencies that implements Plan S for immediate open access to scientific publications. The UNESCO Chair on Open Science at the Université de Montréal works with 17 partners from 10 countries to advance understanding of open science and its impact on knowledge dissemination.

 

In addition to these collaborative efforts, Canada is an active member of the G7 Open Science Working Group, established in 2016 during the G7 Ministers of Science and Technology meeting. The working group shares open science policies, explores incentive structures and identifies good practices for access to publicly funded research.

 

Canada does not report involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been shared with the departments under the Ministry of Science and Technology and the Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser to the Government of India.

Awareness-raising activities on open science have combined recurring domestic events with internationally oriented gatherings. The Centre for Policy Research (CPR) at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc), under the auspices of the Department of Science and Technology (DST) within the Ministry of Science and Technology, organises annual events during International Open Access Week to promote open access to research. In April 2024, the Asia Open Access Meeting in New Delhi, organised by the Confederation of Open Access Repositories (COAR) with UNESCO, addressed strategies for advancing open science. A major outcome was an agreement between COAR and the Information and Library Network (INFLIBNET) Centre to collaborate on India's open science repository network. A virtual panel on genomic data governance, co-organised by the Open Science South Asia Network and the DST-CPR at IISc, took place in May 2024 as a co-located event of the Research Data Alliance's (RDA) 22nd Plenary. In March 2024, the Narsee Monjee Institute of Management Studies (NMIMS), a deemed university in Mumbai, hosted an International Conference on Open Science as part of the ERASMUS Open Asia Project, involving partner universities from India, Finland, the Netherlands, Malaysia and Slovenia.

India's Science, Technology and Innovation Policy (2013) does not, according to the report, include any open science elements. Three national-level frameworks address open science separately. The DST and Department of Biotechnology (DBT) Open Access Policy (2013) mandates green open access. The Scientific Research Infrastructure Sharing Maintenance and Networks (SRIMAN) Guidelines (2022), developed by DST, promote efficient utilisation and wider access to research infrastructure across the country. The third, the Scientific Social Responsibility Guidelines (2022), released by DST, is also cited as addressing elements of open science, such as shared infrastructure and knowledge resources.

 As a specific policy instrument to promote open science, the One Nation One Subscription (ONOS) plan was approved in November 2024 as a Central Sector Scheme with an allocation of approximately 6,000 crore Indian rupees for 2025–2027. It aims to provide country-wide access to high-impact international scholarly research articles and journal publications for students, faculty and researchers at all higher education institutions managed by central and state governments and at central government research and development institutions.

 The report does not indicate the availability of a national implementation plan or roadmap for open science, institutional policies, dedicated funding mechanisms, the integration of open science practices into existing research funding, equitable public-private partnerships, or a national monitoring framework for open science.

India reports the availability of national open science infrastructures comprising federated information technology infrastructure, including high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage. An example is the National Supercomputing Mission (NSM), which envisages a grid of more than 70 high-performance computing facilities networked over the National Knowledge Network (NKN), serving academic and research and development institutions across the country. In addition, the Indian Science, Technology, and Engineering Facilities Map (I-STEM) interactive web platform is designed to help researchers and innovators discover and access publicly funded scientific equipment across the country. At the institutional level, some institutions maintain repositories for research articles and theses, such as the IISc ePrints repository, though the report notes that public access is limited by journal embargo policies. Some institutions are working on maintaining and sharing research data, with no further details provided. In addition, the report cited the Citizen Science for Biodiversity (CitSci India) platform, which features citizen science projects from various institutions.

 India does not report hosting or funding federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, and the report indicates uncertainty about whether existing international open science infrastructures are accessible to Indian researchers. No involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms is reported.

The report indicates that systematic capacity building on open science has not taken place in higher education or research institutions, and that no capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have been conducted. A framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report.

 Open educational resources are reported to be used partly as an instrument for open science capacity building, though no further details are provided. The report does not describe initiatives to support science communication aligned with open science values and principles.

The report does not describe initiatives at the national or institutional level to review existing research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles and values. However, with respect to career evaluation and progression, the report cites the Scientific Social Responsibility Guidelines (2022), though no further details are provided.

 The report does not describe initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices. No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates the lack of initiatives at the national or institutional level that promote innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase the openness of the scientific process. However, as noted above, citizen science projects in relation to biodiversity are conducted across various institutions.

International scientific collaborations on open science are facilitated through the DST-CPR at IISc, which engages in collaborative research with international institutions and participates in international workshops on science, technology and innovation policy and open science. The DST-CPR received funding from Code for Science and Society, a United States-based organisation, to promote open science and organised the Open Science South Asia Network Conference in 2022, described as the first of its kind in South Asia, to cultivate a network committed to open science practices.

 The report does not describe a formal strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science or involvement in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted by the Ministry of Research and Higher Education and the National Research Fund (Fonds National de la Recherche, FNR).

 The FNR organised awareness-raising activities including workshops, stakeholder meetings and PhD training sessions; open science is also a recurring topic in the FNR's annual funding programme webinars. The University of Luxembourg, the country's only public university, and three public research institutes (the Luxembourg Institute of Science and Technology, the Luxembourg Institute of Health and the Luxembourg Institute of Socio-Economic Research) engage in related activities both collaboratively and individually. For example, the Luxembourg Learning Centre, a library at the University of Luxembourg, organises awareness events during International Open Access Week in October and Love My Data Week in March. International partnerships also supported awareness raising, as with the Open Data Days held in March 2025 in cooperation with the Publications Office of the European Commission.

Luxembourg's National Research and Innovation Strategy (2020) includes elements such as open access to scientific knowledge, open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems. The report indicates that national policies, in general, only partly address open science. A National Open Access Policy was adopted in 2015. The FNR maintains its own Open Access Policy for FNR-funded research aligned with the 2021 Recommendation, which was revised in January 2025 owing to concerns about the high costs of open access publishing: whereas open access had previously been mandatory for FNR-funded publications, the revised policy defers to each researcher's institutional requirements. Together, the National Open Access Policy and the FNR Open Access Policy are identified in the report as the principal frameworks guiding Luxembourg’s national implementation of open science. Complementing these efforts, the FNR has adopted a Policy on Research Data Management, which requires all funded projects to include data management plans.

Regarding dedicated national funding mechanisms for open science, the FNR has established an Open Access Fund to cover article processing charges. In addition, through consortium licences involving public research institutions and the National Library of Luxembourg, transformative agreements have been concluded with various publishers. The FNR PSP (Promoting Science to the Public) funding programme supports projects that bring science closer to society through interactive engagement, while the FNR RESCOM funding programme supports communication aimed at disseminating and exchanging scientific outcomes.

At institutional level, the University of Luxembourg introduced a deposit mandate in 2012, requiring researchers to deposit peer-reviewed articles and conference proceedings as open access in its repository.
 
A national monitoring framework for open science is reported as under development. Multi-annual performance agreements between the State and public research institutions require the latter to implement the National Open Access Policy, thereby supporting the tracking of progress. The University of Luxembourg's Open Science working group participates in UNESCO working groups on open science monitoring indicators.

According to the report, Luxembourg's open science infrastructures span federated information technology services and community-managed systems. The National Library of Luxembourg operates a digital library accessible to institutional members and the public. The University of Luxembourg maintains its open repository ORBilu, alongside Melusina Press , a diamond open access university publishing house. The Luxembourg Institute of Health (LIH) and the Luxembourg Institute of Socio-Economic Research (LISER) operate  research portals providing access to their publications, most of which are open access.

 

Data-focused infrastructure includes the Luxembourg National Data Service (LNDS), a government-created organisation that supports the secondary use of public sector data, including data from research institutes, and coordinates the Data Management National Community of Practice, which is open to all professionals with an interest in data stewardship. The national Luxembourg Open Data platform complements these services.

 

Luxembourg hosts a federated international information technology infrastructure for open science through its participation in the European High Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (EuroHPC), within which it operates the Meluxina supercomputer. EuroHPC policies require open science practices as a condition of access to its computing facilities.

 

The report does not describe involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use.

Systematic capacity building on open science takes place across higher education and research performing institutions through both formal and informal activities. At the University of Luxembourg, the Open Science working group mandated by the rectorate delivers training to PhD students at least twice yearly on open access, copyright, intellectual property and data management. Participants receive European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System (ECTS) credits for these sessions. The Luxembourg Learning Centre at the University of Luxembourg provides additional training on information literacy, ethical use of information and open science. The LNDS has joined the Skills for the European Open Science Commons (Skills4EOSC) network of competence centres. LIST established a dedicated research data management team in January 2025 to provide data stewardship, Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) data management planning and training. LISER has integrated open science into its annual institutional training plan and, through its Information Systems division, provides tools for data sharing and reuse while maintaining compliance with personal data protection requirements.
 
Open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula through the formal PhD training described above. Open educational resources are reported as partly used for open science capacity building, though no further details are provided.
 
The report does not describe capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies.
 
Several initiatives support science communication in line with open science values. For example, the FNR operates the Science in Society and the Pairing Scheme: Politics Meets Research programmes, which pairs researchers with decision-makers. The FNR and the University of Luxembourg jointly published a guide on science communication essentials. A dedicated scholarly communication librarian at the Luxembourg Learning Centre supports researchers throughout the research cycle.

At both national and institutional level, initiatives have been taken to review research assessment and evaluation processes. The FNR and the University of Luxembourg have signed the San Francisco Declaration on Research Assessment (DORA) and the CoARA agreement. The University has adopted a CoARA action plan, and Special Advisors for Research Assessment and for Open Science and Data Management have been appointed to drive internal reflection on these topics.

 

Specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression are in place. Under the Law of 27 June 2018 on the organisation of the University of Luxembourg, the tasks of professors include teaching, research, knowledge transfer, dissemination of research results and national and international cooperation, all equally valued in hiring and promotion procedures. The FNR has implemented the narrative CV in its funding application procedures, which incorporates open science aspects. The report cites these same measures as its initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices.

 

Unintended negative consequences of open science have been reported. Open access publishing has led to significant increases in publication costs for research institutions due to article processing charges. In response, the FNR adapted its Open Access Policy to allow greater flexibility in publishing choices while continuing to promote open access.

At institutional level, initiatives are reported that promote innovative approaches at different stages of the scientific process. These address the promotion of preprints, the valuing and sharing of negative scientific results and associated data, participatory methods such as citizen science and crowdsourcing, and open innovation practices. The report does not provide further details on these initiatives.

Luxembourg promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through several memberships and networks. It holds membership in the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Association and participates in ELIXIR, the European infrastructure for life science information, through the Luxembourg ELIXIR node. The FNR is a member of cOAlition S, the international consortium implementing the Plan S open access initiative, and a signatory of the Berlin Declaration on Open Access to Knowledge in the Sciences and Humanities. Both the FNR and the University of Luxembourg are signatories of DORA and CoARA, while the FNR and LISER are members of Open Researcher and Contributor ID (ORCID). The University of Luxembourg participates in UNESCO open science monitoring working groups and in the European Association of Research Managers and Administrators (EARMA) Open Science community of practice. It collaborates with the universities of Mons and Liège in Belgium on the ORBi/ORBilu institutional repository for research publications and engages in informal discussions on open science with the University of Saarbrücken and the University of Lorraine in the Greater Region.

 

The report indicates that Luxembourg does not have a specific formal strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science but notes active engagement through the memberships and partnerships described above. The report does not describe involvement in discussions on regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted to the Ministry of Digital Development, Innovation and Communications, state-owned research institutions and state-owned universities.

 Several awareness-raising activities on open science were organised or planned by the end of 2025. The 8th National Conference of Mongolian Young Scientists, held in 2024 under the theme "Open Science – Youth Participation", addressed the conceptual renewal of science in Mongolia, human resource development and cooperation between state and private sectors. Electronic Information for Libraries (EIFL) organised an Open Access Seminar in Mongolia in 2025, covering the publication of open access journals and books, the development of open access repositories and strategies for collective advocacy.

The report states that Mongolia does not have a comprehensive national policy, strategy or implementation plan for either science, technology and innovation or open science. However, the government and relevant ministries are described as working toward developing such frameworks in consultation with stakeholders and in alignment with international standards. Open science elements are addressed in the Law on Science and Technology, renewed in 2024, through provisions on transparency, open access to scientific knowledge, data sharing and collaboration among research institutions, supporting the development of an enabling environment for open science practices. Specific policy instruments to promote open science are reported as under development.

 At the institutional level, the National University of Mongolia, the University of Finance and Economics and the Mongolian Academy of Sciences have begun implementing strategies to encourage open access to research and data sharing.

Even though Mongolia does not yet have funding mechanisms specifically and exclusively dedicated to open science, the government supports infrastructure development and capacity building initiatives, including the development of institutional repositories and research information systems. Existing research grants, administered through the Ministry of Education and Science, the Ministry of Economy and Development and the Mongolian Foundation for Science and Technology, support activities aligned with open science such as open access publishing and collaborative research.

Efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships for open science include the Public-Private Partnership Law enacted in December 2023 and a national training on science, technology and innovation parks co-organised with UNCTAD in April 2025. In addition, the Mongolian National Chamber of Commerce and Industry and the Science Park are collaborating to facilitate exchange of scientific information and research findings between scientific organisations and businesses.

 The report does not describe a national monitoring framework for open science.

Open science infrastructures in Mongolia include institutional repositories, particularly at the National University of Mongolia, the University of Finance and Economics and the Information Technology Center of the Mongolian Academy of Sciences, which provide open access to scientific publications and research data. The report also identifies platforms for exchanges and co-creation of knowledge between scientists and society as a category of infrastructure present in the country. The Ministry of Economy and Development, in collaboration with the Mongolian Academy of Sciences, is establishing a High-Performance Supercomputing Center to support data analysis and research in areas including artificial intelligence and machine learning, and to provide advanced research technology for scientists and students.

 The report states that Mongolia does not host or fund any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Mongolia is also not reported as being involved in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms.

A framework of open science competencies has not yet been formally incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report. However, the Mongolian University of Science and Technology (MUST) established the Open Education Center in 2019, which develops Massive Open Online Courses, Open Educational Resources (OER) and blended learning models. In 2015, UNESCO and MUST launched an online course in Mongolian on new technologies in effective teaching and learning in higher education, which includes modules on OER and open licensing.

The Mongolian Cabinet approved a resolution in May 2024 to establish a new university under the Mongolian Academy of Sciences. This university aims to prepare specialised human resources in leading fields of science and technology and to intensify interdisciplinary research and development, offering courses on technology transfer and the application of research into production and services; however, the information provided in the report does not clarify whether open science is expected to be explicitly integrated into these courses. 

 At the policy level, the report indicates that capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have not yet been systematically implemented. Some indirect exposure has occurred through international cooperation, workshops and participation in regional forums related to open science and digital transformation.

 Open educational resources are reported as partly used as an instrument for open science capacity building, though the report does not elaborate further.

 The report does not describe initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values and principles.

The report identifies the development of participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities and the promotion of open innovation practices as areas of activity, but provides no descriptive detail on specific initiatives or their implementation level.

Mongolia reported that initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes has been initiated. In April 2025, the Government approved three new regulations under Government Resolution No. 43: "Evaluation Procedures", "Monitoring Procedures" and "Evaluation and Review of Organizational Activities and Results". These aim to ensure regular measurement of implementation processes and to evaluate results based on research and analysis. However, the information provided in the report does not clarify whether open science is explicitly integrated into these initiatives.

 The report indicates that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems.

 Several initiatives were reported to recognise and reward open science practices, including the 8th National Conference of Mongolian Young Scientists in 2024 themed "Open Science – Youth Participation" (with research grants awarded) and the annual Innovation 7 Days event (with Best Paper Awards). However, no further details were provided on how open science is incorporated into these initiatives.

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

Mongolia promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through bilateral and multilateral agreements. Research institutions collaborate with partners in Japan, South Korea, China, Vietnam and Russia in areas including sustainable development, environmental science, digital innovation and climate change. Mongolia also participates in regional and global scientific address whether initiatives under UNESCO and other international organisations. The report indicates, however, the absence of a formal strategy in Mongolia to stimulate cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science.

 Mongolia is not reported as being involved in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted to the Ministry of Education, the Higher Education Council (HEC), the Nasser Centre for Science and Technology (NCST), the Labour Fund (Tamkeen), the Ministry of Labour, higher education institutions, the Bahrain Centre for Strategic, International and Energy Studies (Derasat), the Information & eGovernment Authority (iGA) and the UNESCO Regional Centre for Information and Communication Technology (RCICT). 

 

Awareness-raising activities on open science have been organised by several entities. For example, the HEC invited higher education institutions to a March 2021 session titled "Open Science: A Gateway to Accelerating Progress on the 2030 Agenda in the Post-Covid-19 Era" held as part of the Arab Forum for Sustainable Development 2021. The RCICT has conducted awareness-raising on open educational resources (OER) and open science with entities in the Ministry of Education, the HEC and private educational institutions.

Bahrain's science, technology and innovation policies address, to some extent, some elements of open science. In particular, the National Strategy for Science, Technology and Innovation, adopted in 2020 under the Ministry of Education, supports open access and public engagement in research. The HEC's National Strategy for Higher Education (2014–2024) addresses open access to scientific knowledge and open science infrastructure. In addition, related provisions can be found in Bahrein’s Open Government Data Policy. 

 

No national policy specifically dedicated to open science or a national implementation plan or roadmap for open science is reported. Cited barriers include limited awareness, a nascent research ecosystem and focus on economic growth and digital transformation. Nevertheless, a targeted policy measure exists in the form of the Ministry of Education’s OER policy (2015), which promotes licensing educational, training and research content under Creative Commons licences and is supported by a dedicated OER committee established within the Ministry of Education. The HEC’s new national strategy for higher education and scientific research for 2025–2035 is expected to strengthen the integration of open science.  


At the level of higher education and research institutions, several have adopted open science practices or initiatives (e.g., Derasat maintains an “E-Library” repository for publications, the University of Bahrain promotes open access through its journals); however, no formal policies were reported. 

 

No specific national funding mechanisms for open science are reported. However, certain open science-related activities in some private higher education institutions, such as Kingdom University, are financed through funds designated for scientific research in general. Despite these efforts, open science practices are not formally integrated into national research funding schemes.  

 

Bahrein has adopted several policed encouraging public-private partnerships for both economic and academic purposes. For example, the HEC's Operational Plan for 2023–2026 aims to create a National Research Network, a public-private partnership connecting researchers, academic institutions and industry stakeholders. However, the information provided in the report does not clarify whether open science is explicitly incorporated into these policies. 

 

The report indicates the absence of a national monitoring framework for open science.

Several national and institutional open science infrastructures are reported. At the national level, the Bahrain Open Data Portal provides datasets across topics such as health, education and foreign investment for research use, while the Geographic Information Systems (GIS) National Portal offers spatial data. The RCICT has launched the "My Digital Library" initiative, which aims to publish schools' OER, as well it operates the "Open Resource Portal in Educational Technology and Creativity", a platform for building research networks in educational technology. The report notes that compliance with Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) and Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility and Ethics (CARE) principles is not explicitly stated for these national portals.  At the institutional level, universities and think tanks have their own dedicated libraries and journals that focus on the exchange of research and dissemination of findings (e.g., Derasat maintains an “E-Library” repository for publications). 
 
Bahrain hosts federated regional information technology infrastructure. For example, Bahrain participates in the Arab States Research and Education Network (ASREN), which supports collaboration among nations of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) in implementing, managing and extending Pan-Arab e-infrastructures dedicated for research and education. 
 
The report does not indicate Bahrain’s involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use for open science platforms.

Capacity building on open science has taken place through several institutions, though it is not reported as systematic. RCICT, in particular, has played an active role in hosting workshops and supporting capacity building on OER. As early as in 2014, the RCICT hosted a regional workshop on OER policies with UNESCO. It has also trained teachers, in collaboration with the Ministry of Education, in developing educational content with Creative Commons licences. The NCST offers specialised training in advanced technologies for teachers and students, though not specifically on digital skills required to use open science infrastructures. The HEC reports that open science has not been a priority for higher education institutions, though the capacity building is expected to be considered in the future. 

 

Capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science into science, technology and innovation policies are not reported. The RCICT is planning such efforts. 

 

Regarding open science competencies in higher education curricula, the HEC reports that research skills are integrated into academic programmes at higher education institutions; however, the report does not specify how these skills are mapped to open science competencies.    

 

OER are used to varying extents for open science capacity building. The HEC reports that educational resources are accessible to academic staff and students at higher education institutions, and the RCICT actively supports OER use for this purpose.  

 

Regarding science communication, several initiatives were reported (e.g., the HEC's promotion of scientific research culture through peer-reviewed conferences attended by researchers, decision-makers and business leaders), but connection to open science is not clearly indicated.

According to Derasat, national policies and institutional priorities continue to focus on advancing research output and building infrastructure for innovation, while the integration of open science into research assessment is considered a future step. 

 

Specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems are not reported as formally adopted. The HEC indicates this is being considered for future collaboration with higher education institutions. The RCICT reported the development of a training programme related to open science for K-12 school teachers that will grant credit hours supporting their career advancement. 

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The HEC reports initiatives to promote innovative approaches for open science across the research cycle, which include exploration of open peer review practices, development of participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities and promotion of open innovation. Derasat reports that cross-sector collaboration between academia and industry is encouraged, though no mandated open science policies are in place. The RCICT supports collaboration among departments within the Ministry of Education and public and private educational and research institutions.

Bahrain promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through several channels. The HEC's National Research Strategy 2014–2024 includes a strategic objective on enhancing collaboration with international academic and research institutions, and the HEC encourages higher education institutions to establish memoranda of understanding with international institutions, focusing on joint research and exchange of findings. The RCICT participates in the Open Education for a Better World (Oe4bw) programme, which supports the development of OER at local, regional and international levels, and it also delivers a free regional training programme on open science accredited by the Ministry of Education.  

 

Bahrain did not report to be involved in discussions on regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The report states that the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not been promoted or shared with relevant ministries and institutions in Benin. 

 

No awareness-raising activities on open science were organised or planned by the end of 2025, according to the report. The General Directorate of Scientific Research and Innovation does, however, organise events for sharing research and innovation results linked to the Sustainable Development Goals, with a particular focus on work by young researchers and innovators. These events aim to improve the dissemination of research results and to strengthen ties between the research community and socio-professional actors.

Benin adopted the National Policy for Research and Innovation for the period 2022–2032, developed by the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research in collaboration with the Economic Commission for Africa and the Agence Universitaire de la Francophonie. The policy references  elements related to open science , including open access to knowledge, open science infrastructure, societal participation and dialogue with other knowledge systems, though these are not explicitly framed in terms of open science.  

 

No national policy or legal framework specifically addressing open science is reported, and no a national implementation plan, strategy or roadmap for open science exists. The report notes that existing policy instruments are not yet specific to open science, and that institutional-level policies and strategies require adaptation to this field. 

 

The report indicates that there are no specific national funding mechanisms for open science and that open science practices have not been integrated into existing research funding. It identifies high publication costs in scientific journals, a lack of training on open science practices and differing standards and norms as barriers. 

 

Regarding equitable public-private partnerships on open science, the report states that provisions are included in the National Policy for Research and Innovation and in the statutes of the Beninese Agency for Research and Innovation (Agence Béninoise pour la Recherche et l'Innovation, ABRI). 

 

No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

The report indicates that Benin does not currently have national or institutional open science infrastructures. It notes, however, that existing facilities, including the Beninese Education and Research Network (Réseau Béninois d'Éducation et de Recherche, RBER) and the Computing Centre of the Institute of Mathematics and Physical Sciences (Institut de Mathématiques et de Sciences Physiques, IMSP) at Dangbo, could be further developed and leveraged for this purpose provided an appropriate regulatory framework is established. 

 

Benin does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Nor does it report involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms. Both areas are described as matters to be considered in future.

The report indicates that systematic capacity building on open science has not taken place in higher education or research institutions, that no capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values and principles into science, technology and innovation policies and strategies have been organised, and that a framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula. All three areas are described as matters to be considered in future. 

 

Open educational resources are reported as being used for open science capacity building, though no further details are provided. 

 

Regarding science communication, the report notes that initiatives exist to support the dissemination of scientific knowledge but that these are not specifically aligned with open science values and principles.

Regarding initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes in alignment with open science principles, the report cites the creation of the Beninese Agency for Research and Innovation (ABRI) and the establishment of projects to valorise research and innovation results in universities. However, the information provided in the report does not clarify whether open science is explicitly integrated into these initiatives.   

 

No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported. The report attributes this to low level of awareness among relevant actors. 

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported. The report notes, however, that sensitive research in areas touching on state sovereignty may present limitations for open science.

At the institutional level, the report identifies initiatives promoting innovative and participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle. These include experimentation with open peer review practices, the development of participatory methods that value contributions from societal actors, and participatory strategies to identify the needs of marginalised communities and bring social issues into research agendas.

Benin hosted an international colloquium, “Open Science in the South”, in Cotonou from 25 to 27 October 2022. The event brought together researchers from around the world to discuss the management and opening of research data in Africa. Benin is also a member of the West and Central African Research and Education Network (WACREN) through the Beninese Education and Research Network (RBER). 

 

Regarding a specific strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, the report states that research partnerships exist but are not specifically oriented towards open science. 

 

On the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, the report states that discussions are under way but no specific mechanism has yet been created.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been disseminated through various channels. The Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation, through its Directorate of Data Governance and Science and Technology Indicators, coordinates related activities under Commitment 3 of the 6th Brazilian Open Government Action Plan. The University of São Paulo (USP) and the Federal University of Minas Gerais (UFMG) have published materials on the Recommendation. 

 

Awareness-raising activities on open science have been organised by several entities. The Brazilian Society for the Advancement of Science (Sociedade Brasileira para o Progresso da Ciência, SBPC) has promoted debates at its annual meetings and organised publications, including a special edition of its Jornal da Ciência newspaper and an issue of its Ciência & Cultura magazine. In 2024, the Brazilian Reproducibility Network hosted a conference on reproducibility in Brazilian research (day 1, day 2). This network also developed recommendations on open and reproducible science for the evaluation of postgraduate programmes at the request of the Brazilian Federal Agency for Support and Evaluation of Graduate Education (known as CAPES, Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior) as well as recommendations for journals. In addition, the Brazilian Institute of Information in Science and Technology (Instituto Brasileiro de Informação em Ciência e Tecnologia, Ibict), in collaboration with Portuguese partners (the Foundation for Science and Technology of Portugal and the University of Minho), co-organise the annual Lusophone Conference on Open Science (ConfOA).

The National Strategy for Science, Technology and Innovation (Estratégia Nacional de Ciência, Tecnologia e Inovação, ENCTI) for 2016–2022 was followed by the 5th National Conference on Science, Technology and Innovation in 2024, preceded by preparatory conferences in all 27 states, which aimed to identify key priorities to be included in the new ENCTI. The outcomes of these discussions were compiled in the “STI Violet Book”, whose Chapter 14 addressed open science and informed the design of the new ENCTI. 

 

No consolidated national open science policy, implementation roadmap or dedicated policy instruments are reported. Brazil has participated in the Open Government Partnership since 2011, and the 6th Brazilian Open Government Action Plan (2024–2027), Commitment 3, includes milestones for designing a national open science policy (Milestone 7) and a corresponding action plan (Milestone 8). 

 

Institutional open science policies exist at several institutions. For example, Oswaldo Cruz Foundation (Fiocruz) has adopted the Open Access to Knowledge Policy and the Policy on Data Management, Sharing and Opening of for Research. The State University of Campinas has its open access policy, and the University of São Paulo has adopted policies covering open access, shared infrastructure and citizen science.  

 

Regarding national funding mechanisms for open science, the report cites the National Institute of Citizen Science (Instituto Nacional de Ciência Cidadã, INCC), which receives funds from the Brazilian Council for Scientific and Technological Development (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico, CNPq) and CAPES. In addition, CAPES Ordinance No. 120/2024 establishes a national framework for covering article processing charges for open access publications by Brazilian researchers. CAPES, the São Paulo Research Foundation (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de São Paulo, FAPESP) and CNPq also co-fund the nationalisation of the SciELO (Scientific Electronic Library Online). Open science practices are not reported as widely integrated into national research funding, though some examples exist: FAPESP requires data management plans in its funding calls and has a policy for open access to publications, while the Serrapilheira Institute attaches the Guide to Good Practices in Open and Reproducible Science to its calls for proposals. 

 

Equitable public-private partnerships for open science are not reported as embedded in existing instruments; the report notes this is under discussion for the next ENCTI. 

 

A national monitoring framework for open science is under development. The Brazilian Open Science Observatory (Observatório de Ciência Aberta do Brasil, OCABr), coordinated by SciELO with CNPq financial support, plans to release its first public version by the end of 2025, with indicators on policy indexing, educational resources, portals and scientific output.

Multiple national open science infrastructures are reported. The National Education and Research Network (Rede Nacional de Ensino e Pesquisa, RNP) began implementing the e-Science Network in 2024, a high-performance infrastructure with a minimum speed of 100 Gb/s for scientific data transfer between research institutions. Ibict develops and maintains several platforms guided by the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) and CARE (Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility and Ethics) principles, including the Brazilian Digital Library of Theses and Dissertations (BDTD), the Brazilian Portal for Open Access Scientific Publications and Data (Oasisbr), the Brazilian Scientific Research Information Ecosystem (BrCRIS) and the dARK Network of persistent identifiers. The Brazilian Biodiversity Information System (SiBBr), coordinated by the Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation, provides national biodiversity data following FAIR principles. The SciELO Open Science Programme with IDEIA (Impact, Diversity, Equity, Inclusion and Accessibility) operates a collection of scientitic journals, a preprints server, a research data repository and an academic books collection.  

 

At the level of universities and research institutes, there are 126 open access repositories for publications and 17 research data repositories. A 2021 agreement between RNP, Ibict, CNPq and research institutions such as the Brazilian Agricultural Research Enterprise (EMBRAPA) and Fiocruz aims to structure a federation of institutional research data repositories, and a subsequent call supported several institutions in implementing their own.  

 

Brazil hosts and funds several federated regional and international infrastructure for open science. For example, the SciELO Network spans 17 countries (within South America, Central America, North America, the West Indies, Western Europe and South Africa), and its secretariat is operated by the SciELO Brazil Collection, a member of the network. The SciELO Network and the European Union-supported BELLA programme exemplify Brazil’s engagement in South–South and North–South–South collaborations around shared infrastructure; the BELLA programme through the deployment of the EllaLink submarine cable has improved connectivity between Latin American and European academic networks.

Several institutions have invested in systematic open science capacity building. Fiocruz offers a Modular Training Programme in Open Science in distance learning mode, open to any citizen; cross-disciplinary postgraduate courses on open science; a Capacity Development Programme on Open Science for its employees; and workshops on research data management. The GO FAIR Brasil Saúde Network, a partnership involving Fiocruz, has offered a Refresher Course in Coordinated Research Data Management annually since 2019. The Federal University of Goiás (UFG) has developed courses on research data management and open science at undergraduate and postgraduate levels, while USP has an Open Science working group that develops recommendations for its community. 

 

No specific capacity building programme for policymakers on integrating open science into science, technology and innovation policies is reported, though training and awareness have been promoted through lectures and meetings. 

 

No national-level framework of open science competencies in higher education curricula is reported. The report notes that curricular reforms are complex, though some institutions, such as Fiocruz and UFG (see above), have begun integrating open science into their curricula. 

 

Open educational resources are not reported as systematically used for open science capacity building at national level. Nevertheless, Fiocruz's Modular Training Programme includes modules on open educational resources and open education. The Brazilian Reproducibility Network regularly curates educational resources on open and reproducible science. 

 

With regard to initiatives on science communication in line with open science principles, one example is funding provided by the Serrapilheira Institute for scientific communication aimed at the general public.

CAPES has reorganised its evaluation processes for postgraduate programmes to reduce dependence on journal-based metrics, incorporating recommendations that include open science; related guidelines were published in 2025. In addition, the Hospital de Clínicas de Porto Alegre, associated with the Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul, has conducted qualitative evaluations of research candidates following the Hong Kong Principles, which promote scientific integrity and open science. 

 

Specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems are not reported at national level. As an institutional initiative to recognise and reward such practices, UFG has included "material resources deposited in scientific production repositories" in its teachers' evaluation criteria. 

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

Several initiatives promote innovative approaches at different stages of the research cycle. The report cited the SciELO Programme, which promotes innovations such as mandatory CC-BY licensing across all journals in the SciELO Brazil Collection, the use of preprints as the starting point of research communication, and the requirement that all articles include a data availability statement. In addition, the Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul adopted a dedicated policy for promoting preprints. Further efforts include the creation of a collection in a research data repository at the Takinahaky Centre for Indigenous Higher Education at UFG, with plans to apply “Local Contexts” labels in line with the CARE (Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility and Ethics) principles to support responsible governance of Indigenous data.

Brazil promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through several channels, including the ConfOA conference, annually organised in partnership with Portugal, the global federation of reproducibility networks, the SciELO Network and LA Referencia. 

 

No specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported. The report notes this matter is under discussion for possible inclusion in the new National Strategy for Science, Technology and Innovation (ENCTI). 

 

Brazil is not reported as presently engaged in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted across a wide range of national institutions. It was communicated to eleven ministries, including those responsible for higher education and scientific research, telecommunications, health, the environment and forestry, as well as both universities (Marien Ngouabi University and Denis Sassou Nguesso University) and multiple national research institutes. The dissemination also reached parliamentary bodies (the National Assembly and the Senate), the Postal and Electronic Communications Regulatory Agency (Agence de régulation des postes et des communications électroniques, ARPCE), the General Delegation for Scientific and Technological Research, professional associations, civil society organisations, student organisations, and the national section of the Organization for Women in Science for the Developing World (OWSD). 

 

Three awareness-raising activities on open science were reported. A seminar on popularising the 2021 Recommendation was held from 8 to 10 May 2023 in Brazzaville. This was followed by a conference on "Open Science in Higher Education: issues, opportunities and challenges" from 2 to 4 October 2024, and a national multi-stakeholder consultation workshop on implementing the 2021 UNESCO Recommendations on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence and on Open Science on 19 December 2024.

The Republic of Congo has a national strategy and action plan for scientific research and innovation that addresses the open science pillars: open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructures, open engagement of societal actors, and open dialogue with other knowledge systems. 

 

The national legal framework for open science consists of four ministerial orders issued between November 2020 and April 2021, which created the National Committee for Open Science in the Republic of Congo, defined its functions and appointed its members. The Committee itself is identified as the specific policy instrument for promoting open science. 

 

However, no national implementation plan, strategy or roadmap dedicated specifically to open science has been adopted. Nor does the report describe institutional-level policies for open science in place within research performing institutions, universities or learned societies. 

 

No specific national funding mechanism for open science has been established. Efforts to promote equitable public-private partnerships have been made, though the report notes these are not specific to open science. 

 

A national monitoring framework for science, technology and innovation exists, operating through the Superior Council for Science and Technology and the Interministerial Committee for Science and Technology, but the report notes it does not yet include indicators specific to open science.

The report states that the Republic of Congo does not yet have national or institutional open science infrastructures. 

 

The country does not host or fund any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Existing regional and international open science infrastructures are reported as not yet fully accessible to all relevant actors in the country. 

 

No participation in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations aimed at optimising infrastructure use or developing joint strategies for shared open science platforms is reported. The report notes, however, that the country's membership in a cross-border digital connectivity initiatives, such as the Central African Backbone (CAB)aand the West Africa Cable System (WACS), could enable shared open science platforms in the future.

The report indicates that capacity building activities related to open science have been organised in higher education and research institutions. However, no capacity building programmes specifically targeting policymakers have been conducted, with insufficient funding and limited human resources cited as barriers. 

 

No framework of open science competencies is reported as having been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, though the report indicates that efforts to do so could be considered by the end of 2025. Open educational resources, as defined in the 2019 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Educational Resources, are not reported as being used for open science capacity building. 

 

Several nitiatives supporting science communication in line with open science values were reported, including a seminar on science journalism and the Congolese Research and Innovation Results Valorisation Days (Journées de valorisation des acquis de la recherche et de l'innovation congolaise, JOVARIC).
The report indicates that innovative approaches for open science have been promoted at different stages of the scientific process, though no further details are provided.

Regarding the review of research assessment and evaluation processes, the report states that the African and Malagasy Council for Higher Education (Conseil africain et malgache de l'enseignement supérieur, CAMES), of which the Republic of the Congo is a member, has established mechanisms for monitoring and evaluating research in conformity with open science principles. Several initiatives by Congolese universities and research institutes to strengthen these mechanisms are reported as planned by the end of 2025. Specific clauses recognising open science practices have according to the report been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems through CAMES. 

 

No national initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported as having been launched in the Republic of Congo. The report notes, however, that CAMES recognises and rewards researchers who have distinguished themselves through knowledge sharing, scientific collaboration and dialogue, although no further details are provided. 

 

The Republic of Congo identifies several types of unintended negative consequences of open science practices, including predatory behaviour, data migration, exploitation and privatisation of research data, high publication fees linked to certain scientific publishing models and potential loss of intellectual property.

The Republic of Congo reported promoting international scientific collaboration on open science through knowledge sharing, collaboration and dialogue among researchers via conferences, colloquia and scientific seminars, although it is not specified whether the open science agenda is explicitly incorporated. 

 

The report states that no specific strategy or plan exists for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, including in relation to exchange of best practices and capacity building. 

 

Discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science have been initiated, notably with the Agence Universitaire de la Francophonie (AUF) and UNESCO in the context of research funding.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Higher Education, Research, Science and Technology (MoHERST). The report does not describe any awareness-raising activities on open science organised or planned by national authorities by the end of 2025.

The Gambia has a National Science, Technology and Innovation (STI) Policy 2013–2024, developed by MoHERST; however, the report does not indicate that this policy includes any open science elements. No national policy or legal framework addressing open science or its components is reported; plans exist to review the National STI Policy before the end of 2025, with the intention of incorporating open science, although funding constraints for the review are acknowledged.

 No specific policy instruments to promote open science and no national implementation plan or roadmap for open science are reported. The report also indicates the absence of institutional-level open science policies at research performing institutions or universities.

 No specific national funding mechanisms for open science are reported, nor have open science practices been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms. The report states that funding mechanisms will be established once a national open science policy is developed.

 No efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported. The report also notes the absence of a national monitoring framework for open science or specific open science indicators within national STI monitoring.

No national or institutional open science infrastructures are reported. The report cites the absence of an open science policy as the primary obstacle.

 The Gambia does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. The report further highlights that existing open science infrastructures at regional and international levels are not accessible to relevant actors in the country.

 No involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms is reported.

No systematic capacity building on open science in higher education or research performing institutions is reported. The report identifies limited institutional readiness as the barrier.

 Capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into STI policies have not taken place according to the report. It notes that such programmes will become relevant as the country proceeds to review its National STI Policy and incorporate open science principles.

 No framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula. The report states that open science is not yet fully embraced at institutional level..

 The report does not describe the use of open educational resources for open science capacity building, nor any initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values.

Regarding the review of research assessment and evaluation processes, the report states that initiatives existed in the past but were not sustained due to funding challenges. Plans to revitalise them are noted.

 No specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. The report cites the lack of national open science policy and national readiness as barriers. No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

No initiatives at national or institutional level to promote innovative or participatory changes at different stages of the research cycle are reported. The report states that open science has not yet been implemented.

With regard to international collaborations on open science, The Gambia cites its collaboration with the Organisation of Southern Cooperation (OSC) to establish an Interdisciplinary Research for Africa Centre in the country.

 No specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported. The report cites funding challenges as the obstacle to developing one.

 With regard to discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, The Gambia reports involvement in such discussions within the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS).
